## Polaris 10-Minute Introduction

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/explainers/10-min

10 min read · Full Introduction

# Polaris 10-Minute Introduction

## Where stablecoins break down

DeFi has had two kinds of stablecoin: centralized-but-scalable and trustless-but-small.

The pattern is structural. A project starts with relatively clean collateral (crypto assets, onchain yield); as it grows, it adds real-world assets, centralized stablecoins, or CEX-dependent yield to keep pace with demand. The economics push that way: T-bills and lending desks are large, reliable, and easy to integrate. Onchain alternatives are harder to scale.

The result: the largest stablecoins in DeFi are managed by entities that can freeze balances, blacklist addresses, and change collateral composition at will. They are useful, but not counterparty-free.

Meanwhile, the trustless designs — LUSD, BOLD — have proven immutable CDPs work. But without a scalable onchain yield source, their growth is constrained, their rates volatile, and they have stayed in a trustless-focused niche.

The open question is whether a stablecoin can be *both* trustless and scalable — one whose yield source grows with adoption, not one borrowed from a centralized counterparty. Polaris is built to answer it.

## The lineage

Polaris is not starting from scratch. It descends from a line of immutable, counterparty-free CDP stablecoins:

![Polaris counterparty-free CDP lineage: SAI (2017), LUSD (2021), BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024), pUSD (2026)](/infographics/polaris-lineage.svg)

- **SAI (2017):** The first single-collateral CDP stablecoin. Proved the model works. Later became multi-collateral DAI, then drifted toward centralized backing — Real World Assets, USDC in the PSM, governance-controlled rates.
- **LUSD (2021):** Immutable, counterparty-free, governance-free. Years with no admin breach. But capital-inefficient: it spent roughly 20% of its life more than 1% above peg, and its yield was limited to redemption fee revenue.
- **BOLD (2024):** Liquity V2 — refined CDP mechanics, multi-collateral, still trustless. A genuine improvement, still yield-constrained.
- **pUSD (2026):** The next chapter. A bonding-curve collateral that solves the overpeg problem and produces yield scaling with DeFi volume, without adding a counterparty.

The lineage's lesson: immutability and counterparty-freedom are achievable and proven — SAI, LUSD, and BOLD showed that. The missing piece has always been a scalable onchain yield source.

## The bonding curve: the engine of everything

The bonding curve is what makes Polaris different from every prior CDP stablecoin.

When you deposit ETH into Polaris, the curve issues you **pETH** at the current price. When you redeem pETH, you get ETH back — at the floor price (at minimum), or the market price (if higher).

### The floor price

The floor price is the minimum exchange rate between pETH and ETH under the bonding-curve invariants. It is designed to rise as swap or conversion fees accumulate and a portion adjusts the floor upward. No normal protocol action lowers it.

The implication: pETH is an ETH proxy with a contract-defined lower bound in ETH terms, not a fixed-price ETH substitute. Its market price can move against ETH, but the floor-to-market ratio frames the curve-implied drawdown before gas, slippage, MEV, smart-contract risk, and chain-liveness risk.

If pETH's market price is 0.95 ETH and its floor is 0.85 ETH, your worst-case redemption is 0.85 ETH. As fees accumulate, that floor may rise to 0.87, then 0.90. It never falls.

### Onchain-only yield

The curve earns fees on every ETH↔pETH swap. Those fees raise the floor. So pETH's yield comes from trading volume — from real onchain activity — not from a T-bill desk or a CEX lending book.

The key insight: **the more DeFi activity flows through Polaris, the higher the floor rises, and the more valuable pETH becomes.** Yield scales with adoption instead of being capped by whatever a counterparty can deliver.

## pAssets: a factory of synthetic assets

With pETH as the collateral primitive, Polaris runs a factory of CDP (Collateralized Debt Position) instances. Each issues a different synthetic asset against the same collateral:

- **pUSD** — pegged to the US dollar
- **pGOLD** — pegged to gold
- **pCHF** — pegged to the Swiss franc
- (additional pAssets are deployable as new CDP instances)

### How a CDP works

You lock pETH in a **trove** and mint a pAsset against it. Your collateral ratio must stay above the minimum threshold for your trove type. If it falls below, your trove is liquidated — the Stability Pool absorbs the debt and takes your collateral at a discount.

The peg is maintained by two opposing arbitrage forces:

**Below peg:** Anyone can redeem pAssets for pETH at face value (minus a small fee). If the system is under-backed, redemptions are scaled down in proportion to the reserve backing. This burns pAssets, reduces supply, and drives the price back up.

**Above peg:** Anyone can open a trove, mint pAssets cheaply, and sell them above peg for a profit. This increases supply and drives the price back down.

### Autonomous interest rates

Polaris sets borrower interest rates without an external price oracle. It uses redemption and minting volume as proxy signals:

- High redemption volume → the pAsset is trading below peg → rates rise to deter new minting.
- Low redemption volume → the peg is healthy → rates fall to encourage minting.

This makes rate-setting self-contained and tamper-proof.

### The Stability Pool

The Stability Pool is the safety net for every CDP. Holders deposit pAssets; when a trove is liquidated, the pool absorbs the debt and takes the collateral at a discount. Depositors earn:

1. A share of borrower interest from all CDPs.
2. Discounted pETH from liquidations.
3. Any additional protocol incentives, if enabled, are expected to come from routed hard-asset revenue rather than POLAR emissions.

It is the primary yield venue for pAsset holders and the mechanism that keeps the CDP system solvent.

## POLAR: stewardship through alignment

POLAR is the protocol's stewardship token. Apart from a one-time genesis allocation of 100,000,000 POLAR minted to the stewardship address at deployment, its issuance is deliberately constrained: **beyond that fixed initial supply, POLAR can only be minted by burning pETH in a conversion auction.** There is no other ongoing issuance path — no investor unlock, no inflationary mint function.

### What burning pETH in a conversion auction does

When pETH is burned:
1. The ETH backing the burned pETH is freed from the bonding curve's floor reserve — a configurable rebate goes to the converter and the remainder is recycled into the system (market-buying pETH to refill the reward buffer).
2. The pETH floor price rises (supply contraction).
3. POLAR is minted to the burner.

All three effects are positive for the system. Burning pETH is the highest-alignment action a user can take.

### vePOLAR and stewardship

A designated stewardship role can steer quantitative parameters — fee and interest-rate settings, the conversion parameters — within the bounds the immutable contracts define. It cannot modify the bonding curve logic, CDP mechanics, liquidation rules, or the fixed collateralization thresholds.

The distinction matters: Polaris has stewardship, not governance. The core is fixed; only the dials within it are adjustable.

## The system end to end

![Polaris system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pAssets, burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH](/polaris-system-v2.svg)

The four-step picture:
1. **Swap.** ETH enters the bonding curve and is issued as pETH at the current price.
2. **Collateralize.** pETH is the only collateral every CDP instance accepts.
3. **Borrow.** A CDP mints a pAsset — pUSD, pGOLD, or any other — against that pETH.
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor, releases ETH, and aligns token holders with the protocol's long-term health.

## The flywheel

![Polaris flywheel: more adoption drives activity, activity generates fees, fees fund conversions, conversions raise the pETH floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting, which drives more adoption](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

> Adoption → activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting → more adoption.

Each component reinforces the others:

- More pAsset CDPs create demand for pETH.
- Demand for pETH generates bonding curve swap fees.
- Fees raise the pETH floor.
- A higher floor makes every existing CDP position safer (the collateral is worth more in ETH terms).
- Safer positions lower the protocol's risk profile, attracting more users.
- More users generate more activity, and the loop continues.

This compounding is structural, not dependent on token emissions.

## Three intended invariants

Invariant | How it is enforced

**pETH floor is designed to rise** | Bonding curve math — no normal protocol action in the intended implementation lowers it
**No counterparty in the collateral stack** | pETH is backed by ETH; pAssets are backed by pETH; nothing else enters
**Immutable core** | Bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in bytecode; there is no admin key and no upgrade function for the core

These are not marketing promises. They are intended invariants that must be verified against the audited production contracts once addresses and reports are published.

## Risk model

Understanding Polaris means knowing what can and cannot happen:

**What can happen:**
- Your trove can be liquidated if your collateral ratio falls below the minimum. You keep any surplus above the liquidation threshold — liquidations are not a total loss.
- Recovery Mode triggers when the protocol's reserve backing relative to total debt (its reserve-to-debt ratio) falls below a threshold. While active, borrowing tightens — actions that increase debt or reduce collateral must meet a higher minimum collateral ratio and may not worsen a position's ratio — but liquidation eligibility stays at the normal minimum collateral ratio, and pure collateral top-ups and repayments remain allowed.
- pETH's market price can fall against ETH and with ETH. The floor defines a curve-level lower-bound quote under stated assumptions; it does not protect against ETH falling against other units.

**What cannot happen by design, once the intended production contracts are verified:**
- No normal protocol action can lower the pETH floor under the intended curve invariant.
- The CDP or bonding curve logic cannot be upgraded.
- Your pAsset balance cannot be frozen or confiscated.

---

Where to go next depends on what you want to do.

- Ready to use it? [Getting Started](/getting-started)
- Want the concept glossary? [Core Concepts](/core-concepts)
- Understand the bonding curve in depth: [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) and [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price)
- Mint your first pUSD: [Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove)
- Earn yield: [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool)
- Build on Polaris: [Develop](/paths/develop)

---

## Polaris 2-Minute Overview

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/explainers/2-min

2 min read · Overview

# Polaris 2-Minute Overview

Polaris is a **self-scaling stablecoin operating system** — trustless, immutable infrastructure for minting synthetic assets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No counterparties.

## One primitive, three products

Everything flows from a single primitive: the **bonding curve**.

**pETH** is ETH deposited into a two-way bonding curve. It is the only collateral Polaris accepts. Its floor price — the contract-defined minimum redemption price under the curve assumptions — is designed to rise. pETH is yield-bearing: swap fees accrue to the protocol and raise that floor over time.

**pAssets** (pUSD, pGOLD, …) are synthetic assets minted against pETH via a CDP (Collateralized Debt Position). You lock pETH; the protocol mints a pAsset into your wallet. No counterparty holds your position. The logic is immutable.

**POLAR** is the stewardship token. Beyond a fixed supply minted once at launch, the only way to create more is to burn pETH in a conversion auction. Through stewardship, POLAR holders steer protocol parameters within the bounds the immutable contracts define — they do not control the immutable core.

## The flywheel

The three products compound:

![Polaris flywheel: adoption drives activity, activity generates fees, fees enable conversions, conversions raise the pETH floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

> More pAsset activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting.

Growth reinforces the system rather than diluting it.

## Why it is different

- **Immutable core.** Bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions are fixed in code. No admin key.
- **Onchain-only yield.** Every yield source (borrower interest, swap fees, conversion gains) is internal to the protocol and grows with DeFi activity.
- **Contract-defined floor.** pETH's floor is designed to only rise under the bonding-curve invariants. Your curve-implied downside in ETH terms shrinks as the floor catches up.

That is the whole system. Three products, one flywheel, one immutable core.

---

**Go deeper →** [5-Minute Presentation](/explainers/5-min) · [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) · [Getting Started](/getting-started)

---

## Polaris 5-Minute Presentation

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/explainers/5-min

5 min read · Presentation

# Polaris 5-Minute Presentation

## The problem

Stablecoins have always faced a structural trilemma: **scalable, yield-bearing, or trustless — pick two.**

The largest stablecoins are scalable and yield-bearing but rely on centralized issuers with freeze and blacklist powers. Trustless designs are counterparty-free but capital-inefficient and yield-constrained. The pattern is consistent: as a stablecoin scales, it drifts toward centralized yield, the path of least resistance.

Polaris is designed so drift cannot happen. Its yield sources are *internal* to the protocol, growing with onchain activity. Its core logic is immutable, with no admin key able to add a counterparty under pressure.

## The counterparty-free lineage

Polaris descends from the line of immutable CDP stablecoins:

![Polaris lineage: SAI (2017) → LUSD (2021) → BOLD / Liquity V2 (2024) → pUSD (2026)](/infographics/polaris-lineage.svg)

Each generation fixed a flaw the previous hit. SAI proved CDPs could work; LUSD proved they could be immutable; BOLD refined the mechanism. Polaris extends the lineage by solving the *yield-scaling* constraint: the bonding curve converts trading volume directly into protocol revenue, so yield grows with adoption rather than being rented from a CEX.

## The three engines

### pETH — collateral with a rising floor

pETH is ETH deposited into a two-way bonding curve. Deposit ETH and you receive pETH at the current price; redeem and you get back ETH through the curve, with the floor as the lower-bound quote under the curve assumptions.

The bonding curve's math is designed to enforce two things:

1. **The floor price rises under normal protocol operation.** As swap fees accumulate, the floor adjusts upward. No normal protocol action lowers it.
2. **pETH is yield-bearing.** Every fee the curve earns narrows the gap between pETH's floor and its market price — real yield from real activity, no counterparty required.

pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts. This is a feature: every pAsset is ultimately backed by ETH, with nothing else in the collateral stack.

### pAssets — synthetic assets from CDPs

pAssets (pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF, …) are synthetic assets minted against pETH collateral via CDPs. You lock pETH in a trove; the protocol mints a pAsset into your wallet.

A pAsset trades near its peg because two forces bound it:

- **Below peg:** Anyone can redeem pAssets for pETH at face value, arbitraging the price back up.
- **Above peg:** Anyone can open a trove and mint more pAssets cheaply, arbitraging the price back down.

Interest rates adjust autonomously: redemption and minting volume serve as onchain proxy signals, no external oracle needed. Polaris is a *factory* of CDPs: the same logic can issue pUSD, pGOLD, or any synthetic asset.

### POLAR — stewardship, not speculation

POLAR is the protocol's stewardship token. Aside from a fixed initial supply minted once to stewardship at deployment, the only way to create more is to burn pETH in a conversion auction — no investor unlock, no inflationary mint.

Burning pETH in a conversion auction does three things at once:
1. Frees the ETH backing those pETH from the bonding-curve reserve — partly rebated to the converter, partly recycled into protocol rewards.
2. Raises the pETH floor (supply reduction).
3. Mints POLAR to the converter.

POLAR confers stewardship: a designated stewardship role can steer a small set of quantitative parameters (fee, interest, and conversion settings), but cannot touch the immutable core — the bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, and redemptions. Stewardship, not governance.

## The system end to end

![Polaris system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pAssets, burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH](/polaris-system-v2.svg)

Four steps:
1. **Swap.** ETH enters the bonding curve and is issued as pETH.
2. **Collateralize.** pETH is deposited as the sole collateral in a CDP.
3. **Borrow.** The CDP mints a pAsset (pUSD, pGOLD, or any other).
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH.

## The flywheel

![Polaris flywheel: adoption drives activity, fees enable conversions, conversions raise the floor, a higher floor strengthens collateral, stronger collateral enables more minting](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

> Adoption → activity → fees → conversions → higher pETH floor → stronger collateral → more minting.

Each component reinforces the others. More pAsset CDPs create demand for pETH; demand generates curve fees; fees raise the floor; a higher floor makes every position safer; safer positions attract more users.

## Three intended invariants

Invariant | Enforcement

pETH floor is designed to rise | Bonding curve math — no normal protocol action lowers it
No counterparty in the collateral stack | pETH is backed by ETH; nothing else enters
Immutable core | Bonding curve, CDPs, liquidations, and redemptions are intended to be fixed in audited deployment code

---

**Go deeper →** [10-Minute Introduction](/explainers/10-min) for full mechanics · [Getting Started](/getting-started) to use the protocol

---

## Polaris User Paths

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths

# Polaris User Paths

Start with the path that matches the action you want to take, but check [Launch Status](/launch-status) first. Polaris is currently public Testnet 2 on Sepolia; production mainnet actions are not yet live.

## Start here if

If you want to... | Read

Verify the official app, network, addresses, and audit status | [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status), then [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification)
Mint pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset | [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets)
Earn pAsset or pETH-denominated yield | [Earn Yield](/paths/earn-yield)
Hold pETH or understand the bonding curve first | [Hold or Use pETH](/paths/hold-use-peth)
Burn pETH for POLAR or lock POLAR | [Convert and Lock POLAR](/paths/convert-lock-polar)
Build an app, indexer, vault, or risk system | [Develop on Polaris](/paths/develop)

## Actionability

These paths are decision guides, not production launch instructions until [Launch Status](/launch-status) lists the official app, production contracts, final parameters, and audit status. For now, builders can plan integrations and test on Sepolia with testnet manifests.

---

## Borrow Polaris pAssets

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/borrow-passets

# Borrow Polaris pAssets

**Who this is for:** anyone who wants to mint pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset against pETH collateral and actively manage that position.

**Prerequisites:** an Ethereum wallet, test assets on Sepolia for Testnet 2 or ETH on Ethereum mainnet after production launch, and a working grasp of overcollateralization and liquidation. If those terms are new, read [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) first.

> **Launch status:** Borrowing is testnet-actionable on Sepolia only when using the official Testnet 2 manifests. Production borrowing is not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the app URL, production addresses, final collateral thresholds, and audit status. Do not connect to a frontend or contract that claims to be Polaris unless it is linked from these docs or another official Polaris channel.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — each step builds context the next one assumes.

1. [Getting Started](/getting-started) — confirms your wallet, network, and launch status so you avoid an unverified deployment.
2. [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) — pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts; you must hold it before borrowing.
3. [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) — explains the CDP model and the peg mechanics that determine your interest rate.
4. [Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove) — the conceptual flow for posting collateral and minting your pAsset.
5. [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) — how collateralization, debt, and interest move over time, and how to stay clear of liquidation.
6. [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) and [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) — the failure states that decide how conservatively to size your position.

## What to verify at launch

- The official app URL.
- The intended network: Sepolia (`11155111`) for Testnet 2, or Ethereum mainnet (`1`) after production launch.
- The pETH, pAsset, trove manager, and Stability Pool addresses.
- Final minimum collateralization, liquidation, redemption, and fee parameters for the pAsset you mint.
- Audit reports and verified contract source.

## Next

- Want yield on the pAsset after minting? Continue to [Earn Yield](/paths/earn-yield).
- Want to understand the collateral first? Continue to [Hold or Use pETH](/paths/hold-use-peth).
- Want the safety checklist? Continue to [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification).

---

## Convert and Lock POLAR on Polaris

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/convert-lock-polar

# Convert and Lock POLAR on Polaris

**Who this is for:** long-term participants who want to acquire POLAR through conversion, lock it as vePOLAR, and steer the protocol within its hardcoded bounds.

**Prerequisites:** pETH to burn in a conversion auction, and acceptance that conversion is one-way — you cannot un-burn pETH. To understand what your burn contributes, read [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) first.

> **Launch status:** Conversion and locking are testnet-actionable only where Sepolia artifacts exist. Production conversion and locking are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, POLAR and vePOLAR addresses, lock durations, voting process, final parameter bounds, and audit status. Do not use an unofficial conversion or locking interface.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — understand the token and the irreversible burn before committing pETH.

1. [The POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token) — POLAR's role and why, after the initial distribution, new POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH.
2. [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions) — the mechanism and its risks; read before burning any pETH to mint POLAR.
3. [Participate in a Conversion](/polar/participate-in-conversion) — the conceptual user flow for a conversion.
4. [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) — locking durations, the rights locking grants, and its limitations.
5. [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship) — the hardcoded bounds your vote operates within; read before voting on any proposal.

## What vePOLAR can and cannot do

vePOLAR can steward bounded parameters, vote on permitted onboarding decisions, and receive a stewarded share of protocol yield. (Staker yield routes as primary interest, in pAsset terms, via the protocol's interest router, not the Fee Router; the Fee Router instead buffers bonding-curve fees and distributes them to troves.) It cannot upgrade contracts, change the bonding curve, change liquidation or redemption logic, create emissions gauges, or mint POLAR except through pETH conversion.

## What to verify at launch

- The official POLAR, conversion auction, and vePOLAR addresses.
- Verified source and ABI for each contract.
- Final lock duration choices and vote-power decay rules.
- Final proposal, voting, quorum, timelock, and execution rules.
- The hardcoded parameter bounds for any proposal you vote on.

---

## Develop on Polaris

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/develop

# Develop on Polaris

**Who this is for:** engineers building an app, indexer, risk system, vault, liquidation monitor, analytics surface, or external market around Polaris.

**Prerequisites:** familiarity with onchain reads/writes (viem, ethers, or equivalent) and the Polaris product model. New to these concepts? Read [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) first.

> **Launch status:** Polaris is in public Testnet 2 on Sepolia, with production mainnet forthcoming. Contract addresses, ABIs, verified source, canonical events, SDKs, subgraphs, and API endpoints for production are not yet published. Build against the documented model, but do not hardcode pre-launch addresses, method names, or event signatures. Track [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) before binding to artifacts.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — model the system before you bind to any artifact.

1. [Core Concepts](/core-concepts) — the system model and its component boundaries.
2. [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts) — the deployment shape (a shared core plus one instance per pAsset), the contract set, and the address, source, and ABI format to verify against.
3. [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security) — the security model and audit status; read before treating a deployment as production-grade.
4. [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification) — the pre-interaction checklist for network, chain ID, addresses, and verified source.

## Build now, bind later

Until deployment artifacts ship, the safest useful work is interface planning:

- Model the product concepts: pETH, pAssets, troves, Stability Pools, Fee Router, POLAR, and vePOLAR.
- Design data schemas around stable conceptual entities, not unpublished function names.
- Build verification gates so your app refuses unverified networks or addresses.
- Keep chain-specific config external and replaceable.
- Track the official docs for the deployment manifest, ABIs, and event catalog.

## Gotchas

- **Testnet addresses are not production addresses.** Treat Sepolia manifests as replaceable fixtures. Never use them as defaults for mainnet.
- **One pAsset means one instance.** A pUSD trove and a pGOLD trove are unrelated positions with different managers, pools, parameters, and risk states.
- **pETH is collateral, not pAsset debt.** Do not put pETH in the same accounting bucket as CDP-minted pAssets.
- **Redemptions are global to an instance.** There is no sorted lowest-ICR queue. If you model borrower exposure, model pro-rata reduction across all open troves for that pAsset.
- **Fee Router pETH rewards and primary-interest splits are separate streams.** Do not assume every yield destination is paid by the same contract or denomination.
- **Final event names are pending.** Build your indexer so event bindings are generated from the published ABI, not copied from draft examples.

## Failure modes to handle

Failure mode | Integration response

Wrong chain ID | Refuse reads/writes and show the expected chain from [Launch Status](/launch-status).
Address missing from the manifest | Disable the feature rather than falling back to a search result or environment default.
Explorer source or ABI mismatch | Treat the deployment as unverified and fail closed.
Stale oracle or unavailable price path | Hide leverage/borrowing actions and show risk state as unknown.
Recovery Mode active | Block or warn on collateral-reducing and debt-increasing flows according to live contract state.
Redemption, liquidation, or conversion quote changes before inclusion | Requote before signing and use explicit user slippage bounds.
Unknown pAsset instance | Do not share pUSD assumptions with another pAsset; load per-instance parameters.

## Worked integration sketch

The exact package, ABI, and method names are pending. The important pattern is a fail-closed deployment gate before any read or write:

```ts
type PolarisDeployment = {
  chainId: number
  contracts: Record<string, `0x${string}`>
  abiHashes: Record<string, string>
}

const EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID = 11155111 // Testnet 2 today; production mainnet is 1 after launch.

  if (deployment.chainId !== EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID) {
    throw new Error(`Unexpected Polaris network: ${deployment.chainId}`)
  }

  for (const [name, address] of Object.entries(deployment.contracts)) {
    const code = await client.getBytecode({ address })
    if (!code) throw new Error(`Missing bytecode for ${name}`)

    const verified = await verifyExplorerSourceAndAbiHash({
      address,
      expectedAbiHash: deployment.abiHashes[name]
    })
    if (!verified) throw new Error(`Unverified Polaris contract: ${name}`)
  }

  return {
    peth: deployment.contracts.peth,
    pUSDManager: deployment.contracts.pUSDManager,
    pUSDStabilityPool: deployment.contracts.pUSDStabilityPool
  }
}
```

Use the same shape for production later, but with Ethereum mainnet chain ID `1` and only after [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) publishes canonical production artifacts.

## Next

- Need the system model? See [Core Concepts](/core-concepts).
- Need artifact expectations (addresses, source, ABIs)? See [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts).
- Need safety gates before connecting to a deployment? See [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification).

---

## Earn Yield on Polaris

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/earn-yield

# Earn Yield on Polaris

**Who this is for:** holders who want protocol-native yield from pAssets, pETH, or locked POLAR — without token emissions, bribes, or offchain backing.

**Prerequisites:** a pAsset to deposit (so most readers come here after [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets)) or pETH, plus awareness that Stability Pool yield carries liquidation-conversion exposure to your principal.

> **Launch status:** Yield flows are conceptual and testnet-actionable only where Sepolia artifacts exist. Production deposits are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production pool addresses, final splits, buffering parameters, and audit status.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order — start with where the yield originates before committing capital.

1. [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — the full source map, so you can judge whether each yield stream is sustainable rather than subsidized.
2. [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) — the primary venue for pAsset yield, and how liquidations can convert your deposit into discounted pETH.
3. [Deposit to the Stability Pool](/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool) — the user flow you will follow once the app is public.
4. [The Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router) — how pETH from fees and conversions reaches the configured rewards receiver (the troves, pro-rata to debt, and/or the share system staked by POLAR holders), which determines your non-pool yield.
5. [Flows](/stewardship/flows) — only if you are an integrator or operator seeking protocol revenue sharing; safe to skip as a plain depositor.

## What yield is not

Polaris yield is not a token-emission program, gauge market, bribe market, T-bill wrapper, or CEX lending strategy. It comes from protocol activity: borrower interest, liquidation gains, bonding-curve fees, conversion gains, and other onchain fees described in the yield docs.

## What to verify at launch

- The official app URL.
- The Stability Pool address for the pAsset you deposit.
- The final interest split and liquidation parameters for that pAsset.
- The Fee Router address and verified source.
- Whether any wrapper vault you use is official, audited, and listed by the project that operates it.

## Next

- Borrow first? Go to [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets).
- Lock POLAR for stewarded yield? Go to [Convert and Lock POLAR](/paths/convert-lock-polar).
- Verify contracts before depositing? Go to [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) and [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification).

---

## Hold or Use Polaris pETH

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/hold-use-peth

# Hold or Use Polaris pETH

**Who this is for:** anyone who wants to hold pETH for its rising floor, use it as collateral, or understand the asset before borrowing against it.

**Prerequisites:** a wallet and the right ETH for the environment: Sepolia ETH for Testnet 2, or ETH on Ethereum mainnet after production launch. No trove or pAsset is required to hold pETH; borrowing is optional and comes later.

> **Launch status:** pETH is testnet-actionable only through the current Sepolia deployment manifests. Production pETH use is not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production bonding-curve address, verified source, final curve parameters, and audit status. Do not trust unofficial addresses.

## Reading sequence

Read these in order to understand pETH and its downside bound before relying on it.

1. [The Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) — how ETH deposits become pETH, and where your exit liquidity comes from.
2. [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) — the contract-defined downside bound in ETH terms under the curve assumptions.
3. [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) — only if you may use pETH inside a trove; explains what changes when pETH becomes collateral.
4. [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — how bonding-curve and conversion activity raises the floor for pETH holders.
5. [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) — read before using pETH as collateral anywhere, inside or outside Polaris.

## pETH roles

Role | What to read

Hold pETH directly | [Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve), [Floor Price](/peth/floor-price)
Borrow against pETH | [Borrow pAssets](/paths/borrow-passets)
Integrate pETH externally | [Develop](/paths/develop), [Core Concepts](/core-concepts)
Verify the asset | [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status), [Safety and Verification](/paths/safety-verification)

## What to verify at launch

- The official pETH contract address and verified source.
- The bonding-curve address on the intended network: Sepolia for Testnet 2, Ethereum mainnet after production launch.
- Final curve, fee, and floor-ratio parameters once published.
- The exit path from pETH back to ETH.
- Any external venue's risk controls before using pETH outside Polaris.

---

## Polaris Safety and Verification

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/paths/safety-verification

# Polaris Safety and Verification

**Who this is for:** every Polaris user and integrator; run this path before connecting a wallet, sending ETH, minting a pAsset, depositing into a pool, locking POLAR, or integrating contracts.

**Prerequisites:** none. Complete this path *first*, before any other journey, because impersonation and phishing are most likely pre-launch.

> **Launch status:** [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) is the source of truth. As of June 8, 2026, public Testnet 2 is on Sepolia (`11155111`) and production Ethereum mainnet (`1`) is forthcoming. Until the launch page publishes official app URLs, production addresses, ABIs, verified source, final parameters, and audit reports, treat every claimed frontend, token, contract, airdrop, or support message as unverified.

## Verification checklist

Work top to bottom; each row names a check and where it will be confirmed at launch.

Check | Where it will be confirmed

Current launch phase and network | [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status)
Official app URL | [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) and [Getting Started](/getting-started)
Deployed addresses | [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts)
Verified source and ABIs | [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts) and block explorer links
Audit reports | [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security)
Final user parameters | The relevant product page and contract reference
Stewardship bounds | [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship)

## Things Polaris will not ask for

- A seed phrase or private key.
- KYC for protocol use.
- A direct transfer to a support wallet.
- Interaction with an unpublished address.
- Trust in a frontend that is not linked from official Polaris channels.

## Risk docs

Read [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) for smart-contract, collateral, liquidation, peg, Stability Pool, pETH, stewardship, and phishing risks. Read [Audits and Security](/resources/audits-security) for the security model and audit status.

## Developer verification

For apps and indexers, add explicit allowlists for network IDs and contract addresses once published. Your integration should fail closed if the deployment manifest, ABI, or verified source does not match the official docs.

---

## Polaris Minting and Troves

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting

# Polaris Minting and Troves

Minting means opening a trove, posting pETH collateral, and drawing a pAsset directly from the protocol. Production minting is not mainnet-actionable until [Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the app, verified addresses, final parameters, and audit status.

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[Minting Polaris pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) | How the CDP model works, how rates respond to peg pressure, and why pETH is the only collateral.
[Open a Polaris Trove](/minting/open-a-trove) | The task flow for acquiring pETH, posting collateral, minting a pAsset, and choosing an ICR buffer.
[Managing a Polaris Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) | How to add collateral, repay debt, monitor interest, and avoid liquidation.
[Polaris Asset Catalog](/minting/passet-catalog) | Which assets are pAsset debt tokens and how pETH differs as the collateral token.

## Launch status

For Testnet 2, use Sepolia (`11155111`) and the current testnet deployment manifests. For production, wait for Ethereum mainnet (`1`) contracts and final borrower parameters.

## Related risk

Borrowers should also read [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations), [pAsset Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions), [Polaris Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), and the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

---

## Managing a Polaris Trove

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/managing-your-trove

# Managing a Polaris Trove

A trove is a living position. After you [open it](/minting/open-a-trove), you can adjust it freely: add collateral, mint more, repay debt, or close it. Everything traces back to one number — your **Individual Collateralization Ratio**.

> **Actionability:** Trove management is testnet-actionable only on Sepolia with test assets and current manifests. Production management is not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production addresses, final parameters, and audit status. Use live app and contract values over examples on this page.

## Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)

Your **Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)** is the value of your trove's collateral divided by the recorded value of its debt:

> ICR = collateral value ÷ debt face value

Collateral value comes from the protocol's collateral pricing path. Debt value is what you owe in that pAsset's reference unit, including accrued interest; it is not the temporary market price at which you might buy the pAsset on a DEX. A trove holding pETH worth \$15,000 against 10,000 pUSD of debt has an ICR of 150%. Higher is safer. Two thresholds matter:

- Below the **minimum ICR**, the trove can be [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).
- [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) draw collateral from every open trove collectively, pro-rata to its recorded collateral and regardless of ICR — a higher ICR protects against liquidation, not redemption.

Your ICR moves on its own as ETH's price moves — you need not touch the trove for it to drift. Managing the trove is mostly about keeping the ICR where you want it.

## Before you adjust a trove

> **Before you act:**
> Check your current ICR, the projected ICR after the transaction, Recovery Mode status, accrued interest, your redemption exposure, and the gas cost of buying or repaying pAssets. If you need to acquire pAsset or pETH first, account for DEX slippage, bonding-curve slippage, MEV, and transaction ordering.

> **Warning:**
> Minting more or withdrawing collateral lowers your ICR. Drop below the minimum and your trove is [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) automatically: your collateral covers the debt plus a liquidation penalty, with no margin call, and only the surplus is returned for you to claim. [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) can also reach your position while it is still solvent — there is no minimum ICR to cross — shrinking your collateral and pETH exposure proportionally without your consent. See the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

## Parameter status

Parameter | Status | Why it matters

Minimum ICR | Pending final per-pAsset values | Defines the liquidation line in Normal Mode
Recovery Mode threshold | Pending final value | When system backing is thin, actions that reduce collateral or add debt face a higher minimum ratio; liquidation eligibility stays at the normal minimum
Borrow-rate formula and bounds | Pending final audited values | Determines how quickly debt grows
Minimum debt / dust rules | Pending final values | Can affect partial repayments, partial redemptions, and closing

## Add collateral

Deposit more pETH into the trove to **raise your ICR** without changing your debt. This is the simplest way to step back from a liquidation or redemption threshold during a drawdown. (Need pETH? Mint it on the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve).)

## Mint more / repay debt

- **Mint more.** If your ICR has room, mint more pAsset against the same collateral. This *lowers* your ICR, so leave a buffer.
- **Repay debt.** Burn pAsset back into the trove to reduce your debt and *raise* your ICR. Repaying is the other lever for recovering a thinning position, and the only way to withdraw collateral.

## Monitor interest accrual

Borrowers pay an [autonomous, market-aware interest rate](/minting/minting-passets) that accrues continuously against your trove. Interest adds to your debt over time, so your ICR drifts *down* even if ETH holds flat and you do nothing. Check your accrued debt periodically and repay or top up so accrual does not quietly walk you toward a threshold.

Collateral pETH also *earns* yield while it sits in the trove, partly offsetting the interest cost; see [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) for how the effective rate is composed.

### Worked example: interest accrual

Suppose your trove owes 10,000 pUSD and the app quotes an 8% annualized borrow rate. As a rough estimate, before rate changes and compounding:

Period | Interest estimate | New debt

30 days | 10,000 × 8% × 30 / 365 = 65.75 pUSD | 10,065.75 pUSD
90 days | 10,000 × 8% × 90 / 365 = 197.26 pUSD | 10,197.26 pUSD

If your collateral value stays flat at \$15,000, the 30-day estimate moves your ICR from 150% to about 149%. The contract may accrue continuously and the rate may change, so use the app's recorded debt for precise decisions.

## Close the trove

Closing is the reverse of opening (**repay debt ⇒ withdraw collateral**):

1. **Repay the full debt.** Burn enough pAsset to clear the recorded debt, including accrued interest. You must hold (or re-acquire) the pAsset.
2. **Withdraw collateral.** With the debt at zero, withdraw your pETH. The trove is closed.

If you do not hold enough pAsset to repay (for example, you sold it), buy it back on the market first.

## How ICR maps to your exposure

ICR | Liquidation exposure | Redemption exposure

Comfortably high | Minimal — survives large ETH drops | Low — redemptions reduce every trove pro-rata to its collateral, and a large buffer keeps your ICR healthy afterward
Moderate | Survives normal volatility | Moderate — a system-wide redemption shrinks your collateral proportionally and can erode your buffer
Near the minimum | High — a sharp ETH move liquidates you | A system-wide redemption reduces your collateral pro-rata and, on thin margin, can leave you closer to the liquidation line

The takeaway: a buffer is not wasted capital, it is the price of staying in control of your position. Keep enough margin that ordinary volatility and quiet interest accrual cannot force an outcome you did not choose.

---

## Minting Polaris pAssets

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/minting-passets

# Minting Polaris pAssets

## The CDP model

Polaris issues pAssets through a **Collateralized Debt Position (CDP)**. From the outside it looks like borrowing against collateral, but underneath it differs from a money market like Aave.

- A **money market** matches lenders and borrowers through a shared pool. You borrow funds another user supplied; an interest rate floats with pool utilization.
- A **CDP** lets each borrower **mint** the pAsset directly from the protocol. You aren't borrowing anyone's deposit; you're your own mini central bank: post collateral ⇒ mint a pAsset ⇒ owe that debt back.

Each position is isolated in a **[trove](/minting/managing-your-trove)** with its own collateral, debt, and collateralization ratio. Users of a given pAsset share only (1) the pAsset and its peg state, and (2) system-wide parameters like the [Total Collateralization Ratio and Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode). That isolation bounds the blast radius: each pAsset keeps its own debt accounting, so trouble in one cannot reach the troves or collateral of another.

### Strengths and trade-offs

The CDP model is ~8 years proven, its strengths and weaknesses well understood; Polaris's design amplifies the former and dampens the latter.

Strengths | Trade-offs

**Trustless** — logic fully enforced onchain, no intermediaries | **Liquidation risk** — collateral below threshold can be [liquidated](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations)
**Transparent** — ratios and liquidation logic auditable onchain | **Volatility** — sharp collateral drops can trigger liquidations
**Optionality** — unlock liquidity without selling your assets | **Smart-contract risk** — mitigated by audits, never zero
**Debt-asset agnostic** — the minted asset can be anything | **Capital inefficiency** — overcollateralization leaves capital idle

## What Polaris does differently

### Collateral is pETH, and only pETH

Polaris does not accept wstETH or other staked-ETH tokens: they would be external dependencies that cap scaling. It accepts only **[pETH](/peth/bonding-curve)**, ETH deposited into the bonding curve. This dampens the CDP model's capital-inefficiency weakness, because pETH is:

1. an ETH proxy with an **ever-rising floor price**, and
2. **yield-bearing** — collateral pETH also earns from other parts of the system (e.g. ETH released by conversions).

### Autonomous, market-aware interest rates

Setting CDP interest rates is where most designs struggle: Maker still uses governance-set rates (spiky, unpredictable); Liquity v1 had none (sustained supply pressure as T-bill rates rose); Liquity v2 pushes rate-setting onto users.

Polaris sets rates **autonomously, without a direct market-price feed for the pAsset**, by reading signals already inside the system:

- **Redemptions are profitable when pUSD < \$1** ⇒ a spike in redemption volume signals a downward depeg ⇒ raise the rate.
- **Minting is profitable when pUSD > \$1** ⇒ a spike in minting volume signals an upward depeg ⇒ lower the rate.

This gives the protocol an internal peg-pressure signal, not a substitute for every oracle in the system. Collateral valuation, non-dollar pAsset reference units, stale-price checks, and liquidation math still depend on the final audited pricing paths for those assets. See [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) for the mechanism behind the peg-pressure signal.

## Ready to mint

Minting a pAsset means opening a trove. See **[Open a Trove](/minting/open-a-trove)** for the steps, and **[The pAsset Catalog](/minting/passet-catalog)** for what you can mint.

---

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Open a Polaris Trove

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/open-a-trove

# Open a Polaris Trove

A **trove** is your individual borrowing position: its own collateral, debt, and collateralization ratio. Opening one is how you mint a pAsset: **connect wallet ⇒ acquire pETH ⇒ deposit pETH as collateral ⇒ choose and mint a pAsset ⇒ confirm ⇒ monitor your ICR**. This page walks each step; for the concepts behind it, see [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) and [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove).

> **Actionability:** This flow is testnet-actionable only on Sepolia (`11155111`) using the current Testnet 2 manifests. Production troves are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production addresses, final parameters, and audit status. Screenshots and exact UI labels are forthcoming; the steps below describe the flow, not the final button copy.

## Before you start

- A wallet with the right asset for the environment: **Sepolia ETH** for Testnet 2 or **ETH on Ethereum mainnet** after production launch.
- A target pAsset in mind — see [the catalog](/minting/passet-catalog).

> **Before you act:**
> Opening a trove creates debt. Check the app's displayed minimum ICR, Recovery Mode status, current borrow rate, your redemption exposure (how a below-peg pAsset reduces every open trove pro-rata), and the pETH you will receive after bonding-curve fees and slippage. Launch parameters are still pending, so use the live app values rather than this guide's examples.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | What to use before launch

Network | Testnet 2: Sepolia (`11155111`); production: Ethereum mainnet (`1`) after launch | [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status)
Minimum ICR per pAsset | Pending final parameters | The value the app and contracts show at launch
Recovery Mode trigger | Pending final parameters | Monitor the app's system-health indicator
Borrow interest-rate formula and bounds | Pending final parameters | The current quoted rate before minting
Redemption fee formula | Pending final parameters | The pre-confirmation quote for any transaction
pETH curve parameters and swap fee | Pending final audited values | The bonding-curve quote before acquiring collateral

## Steps

### Connect your wallet

Open the Polaris app and connect.

### Acquire pETH

Deposit ETH into the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) to receive pETH. pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts. If you already hold pETH, skip this step.

### Deposit pETH as collateral

Choose how much pETH to lock in the trove. This collateral stays in the trove until you withdraw it.

### Choose pAsset and amount

Pick the pAsset (pUSD, pGOLD, …) and the amount of debt to take on. The app shows the resulting **Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)** — your collateral value divided by your debt value — before you confirm.

### Confirm

Sign the transaction. The minted pAsset arrives in your wallet; the debt is recorded against your trove.

### Monitor your ICR

Your ICR moves with the price of ETH and with any changes you make. Watch it from the trove dashboard and adjust before it nears a threshold. See [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove).

## Choosing a safe collateralization buffer

Your ICR is the single number that determines your risk. Two distinct events depend on it:

- **[Liquidation](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).** If your ICR falls below the minimum, your trove is liquidated and you lose collateral to cover the debt (plus a penalty). A low ICR is most dangerous when ETH drops sharply.
- **[Redemption](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions).** When the pAsset trades below peg, arbitrageurs redeem it for pETH. A redemption applies collectively across all open troves in proportion to their recorded collateral, not to a single lowest-ICR position, so it reduces every trove's collateral and debt together regardless of ICR. Being redeemed against carries no liquidation penalty, but it shrinks your position and your directional exposure without your consent.

A larger buffer above the minimum keeps you clear of liquidation through volatility. Redemptions are different: they reach every open trove proportionally regardless of ICR, so a buffer does not keep you out of them, but a healthy ICR ensures a redemption does not leave you near the liquidation line. The right buffer depends on how volatile you expect ETH to be and how actively you intend to manage the trove — a position you watch daily can run leaner than one you set and forget.

> **Warning:** There is no margin call and no grace period. If ETH moves against you faster than you top up, liquidation is automatic. Size your buffer for the worst day you are willing to sit through, not the average one.

## Worked example: ICR buffer

The numbers below are illustrative only. They show the math; they are not launch parameters.

Assume the app displays a 120% minimum ICR for a pUSD trove, and you open with \$18,000 of pETH collateral against 10,000 pUSD of debt:

Step | Collateral value | Debt | ICR

Opening position | \$18,000 | 10,000 pUSD | 180%
After a 20% collateral-value drop | \$14,400 | 10,000 pUSD | 144%
After the same drop plus 200 pUSD of accrued interest | \$14,400 | 10,200 pUSD | 141%

That buffer survives the modeled move. Had the same user opened at 135%, a 20% collateral-value drop would take the trove to about 108%, below the illustrative 120% minimum. The lesson is not that 180% is the right number; it is that your buffer has to cover price moves, interest accrual, gas delays, and the way redemptions reduce every open trove's collateral and debt collectively, pro-rata to collateral and regardless of ICR.

---

## Polaris Asset Catalog

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/minting/passet-catalog

# Polaris Asset Catalog

A **pAsset** is a synthetic debt asset minted from a Polaris CDP against pETH collateral. Each pAsset is overcollateralized, counterparty-free, and redeemable through onchain protocol mechanics under the usual assumptions: functioning contracts, live Ethereum execution, available gas, and valid pricing data. **pETH is different:** it is not minted from a trove; it is the bonding-curve collateral token that backs pAssets. The catalog is open: through [StablecoinOS](#stablecoinos-an-open-catalog), credible teams can add new pAssets, all sharing the same pETH collateral and bonding curve.

## Polaris assets

Asset | Tracks | Notes

**pUSD** | US dollar | The flagship pAsset. Counterparty-free dollar with yield that scales with protocol activity. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).
**pETH** | ETH | *Special.* Not minted from a trove: it is the collateral itself, produced by the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) with an ever-rising floor price. It backs every other pAsset.
**pGOLD** | 1 ounce of gold | Decentralized gold exposure, backed by pETH rather than bullion. See the comparison below.
**pCHF** | Swiss franc | A non-USD fiat peg, demonstrating that the model is debt-asset agnostic across currencies.
**pBigMac** | The [Big Mac Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Mac_Index) | A novelty pAsset tracking a Big Mac's price, proof that Polaris can mint anything with a price feed and a flexible enough fee flow.

## pGOLD vs centralized gold (XAUT/PAXG)

pGOLD and the incumbent gold tokens both give gold-price exposure, but they are structurally different products.

Property | pGOLD | XAUT / PAXG

Collateral | pETH, fully onchain | Physical gold in custody
Redemption | Permissionless, no protocol-level minimum published yet | Issuer-gated; account requirements and redemption minimums depend on the issuer's current terms
KYC | None | Required to redeem
Physical custody | None — nothing to store or insure | Vaulted bullion, with custody risk
Counterparty | Polaris protocol only | Tether / Paxos as issuer

The trade-off is honest: pGOLD inherits ETH-collateral and smart-contract risk where XAUT/PAXG inherit custodial and issuer risk. What pGOLD removes is the *permissioned* layer: no counterparty can freeze, blacklist, or gate your redemption.

## StablecoinOS: an open catalog

The CDP model is **debt-asset agnostic**: it can mint a synthetic pegged to any asset with robust reference pricing and risk parameters. Most protocols ship one stablecoin and stop. Polaris is built as a *factory* of CDP instances under **StablecoinOS**: one CDP per pAsset, all sharing the same pETH collateral pool and bonding curve.

That shared collateral does double duty. It lets credible teams add a new pAsset (pEUR, pSilver, or something stranger) without bootstrapping a fresh collateral base. And because each instance has its own debt asset, a violent day in one market (a gold spike, say) drives liquidations on that instance while the others barely move. The pETH collateral is mutualized; the risk is not.

The result is a catalog that grows without diluting the safety of what already exists.

---

[pGOLD: Finishing What DigixDAO Started](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pGOLD-finishing-what-digixdao-started)
How pGOLD breaks the XAUT/PAXG duopoly without touching a single gold bar.

## Polaris pETH and Bonding Curve

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/peth

# Polaris pETH and Bonding Curve

pETH is ETH deposited into the Polaris bonding curve. It is not a CDP-minted pAsset; it is the collateral token that backs pAssets.

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[pETH Bonding Curve](/peth/bonding-curve) | How ETH becomes pETH, how the two-way curve works, and what contract-native liquidity assumes.
[pETH Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) | What the floor price is, why it is designed to rise, and how the floor ratio frames ETH-denominated downside.

## Launch status

The current public phase is Testnet 2 on Sepolia. Production pETH use is not mainnet-actionable until [Launch Status](/launch-status) links the official app, production bonding-curve address, verified source, final parameters, and audit status.

## Related pages

- [Hold or Use pETH](/paths/hold-use-peth) for the user path.
- [Minting Polaris pAssets](/minting/minting-passets) for using pETH inside troves.
- [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) for collateral, execution, and smart-contract risk.

---

## pETH Bonding Curve

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/peth/bonding-curve

# pETH Bonding Curve

The bonding curve is the foundation of Polaris. From this one primitive come pETH, its rising floor, non-liquidatable ETH loans, and value capture on every interaction.

## What is a bonding curve?

A two-way bonding curve lets you swap like a regular AMM pool, with one key difference: **it needs no liquidity providers.** Instead, it enforces a mathematical relationship between a token's price and its supply:

- Demand rises, tokens are minted, price increases.
- Tokens are burned/sold, price decreases.

Buying adds ETH to the reserve and **mints** pETH. Selling withdraws from the reserve and **burns** pETH. The result is contract-native liquidity with no external liquidity provider, subject to Ethereum liveness, available gas, MEV, slippage, and the bonding-curve contracts working as designed. You have used bonding curves without knowing it: every AMM is one (Uniswap, Curve), and Pump.fun is a well-known example.

Two things make pETH's curve different from Pump.fun's:

1. **Purpose**: Pump.fun raises capital to seed liquidity; Polaris uses the curve to *back* pETH permanently.
2. **Temporality**: Pump.fun's curve terminates at "graduation"; the pETH curve is designed to keep running.

## What's unique about pETH's curve

pETH's curve is **two-way** (ETH ⇒ pETH and pETH ⇒ ETH) and **enforces a rising price floor** for pETH, measured in ETH, under the curve's audited invariants. Two mechanisms lift the floor and none normally lowers it: swap fees, charged in pETH and periodically burned, and [conversion auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions) that burn pETH to mint POLAR. See [The Floor Price](/peth/floor-price) for how it only rises.

![pETH bonding curve: ETH deposits mint pETH along a power-law curve with a rising floor price](/infographics/polaris-bonding-curve.svg)

A rising floor is more than a safety hatch for holders. It lets the **whole protocol capture value on each interaction**, and defines a reserve-backed exit path while the contracts are live, Ethereum is processing transactions, and users can pay execution costs.

### The maths

The curve follows the formula:

$$
P = \alpha \cdot Q^{\beta}
$$

- **α**: a scaling parameter of little importance.
- **Q**: the supply of pETH.
- **β**: the power-law exponent that sets the curve's behaviour.

As supply `Q` increases, the price of pETH (in ETH) increases. **β** sets how tightly pETH tracks ETH: a large β decouples pETH and makes it volatile; a small β couples them closely. Polaris will use **β around 0.3**: close coupling, with room for interesting dynamics.

> *The exact final β is disclosed closer to launch.*

## pETH beyond the CDP

pETH is tailor-made collateral for Polaris CDPs, but it has a life of its own.

### ETH loans (pETH loops)

You can supply pETH back into the bonding curve and **borrow the floor-price value in ETH** from the reserve. These loans are:

- **Non-liquidatable by price movement**: there is no collateral-price liquidation trigger.
- **Withdrawable through repayment**: repay the ETH owed and reclaim your pETH while the contracts and Ethereum are live.
- **Recorded onchain as an NFT.**

Because the loan is sized to the floor price, it is designed to stay solvent for the protocol under the floor-price invariant: a borrower who never repays has effectively sold pETH to the curve at floor price, a good deal for the curve if pETH trades at a premium to its floor. Looping pETH gives **leveraged ETH-aligned exposure to Polaris's growth without touching anything but ETH.**

### DeFi composability

pETH's rising floor and eventual deep ETH backing could make it a prime asset *outside* Polaris too. A pETH/ETH market on Aave, Morpho, or Euler is a natural fit, much like stETH across DeFi today.

## pETH vs stETH

stETH is a prime asset, but it lacks contract-native liquidity from its own reserve. Native withdrawals depend on the validator exit queue and can vary; DEX liquidity is deep, but depends on liquidity providers who can stop supplying in a panic.

**pETH** | **stETH**

Liquidity | Bonding-curve reserve, subject to contract and chain liveness | Deep, but LP-dependent
Withdrawal | Onchain sell path, with gas/slippage/MEV | Validator-exit queue; timing varies
Price floor | Contract-enforced under audited invariants | None
Main risk | Price risk vs ETH, bounded by the floor under the curve assumptions | LP/liquidity risk

> stETH is a prime asset, don't get us wrong. It just doesn't provide this kind of reserve-backed onchain sell path.

## Risk overview

pETH carries three main risks:

1. **Technical risk**: the bonding curve is a smart contract; technical risk is unavoidable, mitigated through audits and modeling.
2. **Execution risk**: using the curve still requires Ethereum liveness, available gas, and transactions that can tolerate slippage and MEV.
3. **Price risk**: pETH is volatile against ETH. Your exposure depends on your entry price relative to the floor, i.e. the [floor ratio](/peth/floor-price): a higher ratio means a smaller curve-implied drawdown before execution costs.

The curve design removes liquidity-provider withdrawal risk. That does not make execution riskless: the curve provides a contract-defined buy/sell path and floor while the relevant invariants hold.

---

[The Bonding Curve: Polaris' Secret Weapon](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve)
How a single mechanism enables pETH's rising floor, contract-native liquidity, and protocol-wide value capture.

---

## pETH Floor Price

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/peth/floor-price

# pETH Floor Price

## What it is

The **floor price** is pETH's mathematically enforced minimum price, in ETH, under the bonding-curve invariants. It is the curve price for the *last person out* if everyone exited the bonding curve at once — assuming the contracts work as designed, Ethereum is live, and users can pay execution costs. Because most exiters get a better curve price, it is a protocol worst case, not an average.

The floor is not a promise or a target defended by a treasury. It is a direct consequence of the bonding curve's reserves and supply, enforced by immutable code and dependent on the audited invariants holding.

## Why it only rises

Two mechanisms push the floor up; no normal protocol action pushes it down:

- **Swap fees feed the burn.** Every swap on the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) charges a pETH fee that accrues to the protocol's Fee Router. The Fee Router periodically floor-sells and burns accumulated pETH, reducing supply against an unchanged-or-growing ETH reserve and raising the floor.
- **Conversion auctions.** Minting POLAR requires [burning pETH](/polar/conversion-auctions), which again reduces supply and lifts the floor.

The result is a staircase under normal operation: pETH's market price fluctuates above the floor, while the floor itself steps upward over time.

## The floor ratio

The **floor ratio** expresses the floor as a percentage of the current market price:

$$
\text{Floor ratio} = \frac{\text{Floor price}}{\text{Market price}} \times 100
$$

It tells you the curve-implied drawdown to the floor before gas, slippage, MEV, and smart-contract or chain-liveness risk. A 90% floor ratio means 10% downside to the floor in ETH terms under those assumptions; 67% means 33% downside.

Floor ratio | Curve-implied drawdown to floor

90% | 10%
67% | 33%
55% | 45%

The floor ratio is itself volatile, driven by market sentiment and activity:

- In phases of **wild growth**, the market price runs far ahead of the floor, so the ratio can fall, sometimes below 50%.
- As the market price **normalizes**, the floor catches up and the ratio rises, until the limited remaining downside makes pETH attractive again.

## Assumptions behind the floor

Assumption | Why it matters

Bonding-curve contracts function as audited | The floor is a contract invariant, not an offchain guarantee
Ethereum remains live | Exiting requires a transaction that can be included
Users can pay gas | High gas can make small exits uneconomic
Slippage and MEV are tolerated or bounded | The quoted curve price and realized execution can differ
The relevant invariants stay unchanged and unbroken | The floor depends on reserves, supply accounting, and burn/mint rules

> Your risk in pETH is decided at entry: the spread between your entry price and the floor (the floor ratio), plus execution and technical risk. Under the intended curve invariant, the floor moves up, not down.

---

[The Bonding Curve: Polaris' Secret Weapon](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve)
How a single mechanism enables pETH's rising floor, contract-native liquidity, and protocol-wide value capture.

## Deposit to the Polaris Stability Pool

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool

# Deposit to the Polaris Stability Pool

This page walks through depositing a pAsset (pUSD in the examples) into the [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool), claiming rewards, and withdrawing. If you have not yet, read [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) first — it explains what you earn and the risk you take on.

> **Actionability:**
> Stability Pool deposits are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia using the current Testnet 2 manifests. Production deposits are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production pool addresses, final reward/withdrawal parameters, and audit status. Screenshots and the exact UI flow are forthcoming; the steps below describe the intended flow and will get images at launch.

## Before you start

You need:

- A pAsset to deposit — most commonly **pUSD**. [Mint pUSD](/minting/minting-passets) first if you hold none.
- ETH for gas.
- An understanding that liquidation gains arrive in **pETH**, and the [risk](/yield/stability-pool#the-honest-risk) of a severe crash. For pure pUSD exposure, use a wrapper vault — see [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources).

> **Before you act:**
> Check the pool's current size, your projected share, accrued reward tokens, withdrawal conditions, and whether you are comfortable receiving pETH during volatile liquidations. If you plan to convert pETH rewards back to pAsset, account for gas, slippage, MEV, and wrapper-vault availability.

> **Warning:**
> A Stability Pool deposit can lose value. Liquidations convert part of your deposited pAsset into pETH, and in a severe or flash crash the pETH you receive can be worth **less** than the pAsset burned — your principal is not protected. Size your deposit accordingly and read the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

## Deposit

### Open the pool

Open the Polaris app and go to the **Stability Pool** for your pAsset (e.g. the pUSD pool).

### Enter an amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to deposit.

### Approve the spend

**Approve** the pUSD spend, if prompted (a one-time token approval).

### Confirm the deposit

Confirm the **deposit** transaction in your wallet.

### Position is active

Your share of the pool is now active. It accrues borrower-interest yield immediately and is eligible for liquidation gains from the next liquidation onward.

## Claim rewards

Your two reward streams behave differently:

- **Interest share (pUSD)** accrues continuously to your position.
- **Liquidation gains (pETH)** accumulate to your position with each liquidation processed.

To realize them:

### Open your position

In the Stability Pool view, open your position to see **claimable pUSD** and **claimable pETH**.

### Claim or compound

Select **Claim** to withdraw rewards to your wallet, or compound them back into the pool where supported.

### Confirm

Confirm the transaction in your wallet.

## Withdraw

### Choose Withdraw

In the Stability Pool view, choose **Withdraw**.

### Enter an amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to remove, or select the full balance.

### Confirm

Confirm the transaction in your wallet.

Withdrawing settles any unclaimed rewards. Your deposited pUSD balance fluctuates as liquidations convert part of it into pETH — what you withdraw reflects the pool's current state, not a fixed principal.

## Next steps

- [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) — the concept, in full.
- [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — every source feeding your yield.

---

## Polaris Stability Pool

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/yield/stability-pool

# Polaris Stability Pool

> **Before you deposit:**
> A Stability Pool deposit is not a fixed pAsset savings balance. Liquidations can burn part of your deposited pAsset and replace it with pETH rewards. Before depositing, check the current pool size, claimable rewards, pETH price exposure, withdrawal conditions, and whether wrapper vaults are available.

## What it is

The **Stability Pool** is a reserve of pAssets — pUSD, for example — that depositors supply to the protocol. It backstops [liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): it holds pUSD on standby so that, when a [trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) falls below its minimum collateralization ratio, the protocol can close out that debt.

Each [pAsset](/minting/passet-catalog) has its own Stability Pool. A pUSD pool backstops pUSD troves, a pGOLD pool backstops pGOLD troves, and so on across the [StablecoinOS](/core-concepts). One pool per instance bounds the blast radius: a depositor is only ever exposed to liquidations of the pAsset they deposited, never to another pAsset's pool.

## How it processes liquidations

Most CDP designs require a liquidator to bring fresh capital to close a bad position. Polaris does not — the capital is already pooled.

When a trove is liquidated:

1. The pool **burns** its pUSD against the liquidated debt, up to the amount available (a minimum buffer is always kept, so the pool is never fully emptied). Any debt the pool cannot cover is **redistributed** to the remaining troves instead.
2. The liquidated trove's **pETH collateral is paid out** to depositors, pro rata to their share of the pool.

Because liquidations carry a penalty, the pETH paid out is worth *more* than the pUSD burned. A depositor's pUSD is converted into pETH at a discount to market — typically a net gain.

> **Note:**
> Liquidation gains arrive as **pETH**, not pUSD. If you want pure pUSD exposure, wrapper vaults that convert that pETH back to pUSD will be available at or shortly after launch. See [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources).

## Why it is the primary yield venue

Depositing in the Stability Pool earns two uncorrelated streams:

Stream | Paid in | Character

**Share of borrower interest** | pUSD | Regular, continuous, non-spiky
**Liquidation gains** | pETH | Event-driven, spiky, arrives with volatility

The interest share is the steady base; liquidation gains spike precisely when markets are turbulent. Together they make the Stability Pool the protocol's **primary yield venue** for pAssets — and the foundation other yield products build on. The share of borrower interest routed to depositors is [stewarded](/stewardship/stewardship) within hardcoded bounds (a floor, to keep the pool funded for liquidations; a ceiling, to keep pUSD liquid across DeFi); exact values are finalized closer to launch.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Borrower-interest share to Stability Pool | Pending final stewarded bounds and launch value | Determines steady pAsset-denominated yield
Liquidation penalty / depositor discount | Pending final values | Determines expected pETH upside on healthy liquidations
Reward claim and compounding behavior | Pending final UI and contract details | Determines whether rewards accrue separately or auto-settle
Wrapper vault availability | Expected at or shortly after launch; final availability pending | Determines whether users can automate pETH-to-pAsset conversion
Withdrawal constraints | Pending final implementation | Determines how quickly and under what conditions deposits can exit
pETH pricing and execution assumptions | Pending final audited paths | Determines realized value when rewards are sold or wrapped

## Worked example: Stability Pool P&L

Suppose you deposit 10,000 pUSD into a 1,000,000 pUSD Stability Pool, giving you 1% of the pool. A liquidation burns 100,000 pUSD from the pool and pays out pETH worth \$110,000 at execution.

Item | Pool-level amount | Your 1% share

pUSD burned | 100,000 pUSD | 1,000 pUSD
pETH received at execution value | \$110,000 | \$1,100
Gross liquidation gain before price movement | \$10,000 | \$100

That is the healthy-liquidation case: part of your pUSD position converted into discounted pETH. If pETH falls 15% before you sell or wrap it, your \$1,100 of pETH becomes \$935 — a \$65 loss versus the 1,000 pUSD burned. Interest income and later liquidations can offset losses, but do not remove pETH price risk.

## The honest risk

Depositing is not free of risk.

> **Warning:**
> In a severe or flash crash, a trove can fall below 100% collateralization before liquidation completes. If that happens, the pETH you receive can be worth **less** than the pUSD you contributed. The liquidation penalty makes a net gain the normal case, not a guaranteed one. Size your deposit accordingly, and see the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

Your liquidation gains are also denominated in pETH, which is [volatile against ETH](/peth/floor-price). The wrapper vaults above mitigate that exposure, but at the base layer you accept pETH.

## Next steps

- [Deposit to the Stability Pool](/yield/deposit-to-stability-pool) — the step-by-step.
- [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources) — every source feeding pAsset yield.

---

[pUSD: No Counterparty, No Ceiling](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pusd-no-counterparty)
How pUSD delivers counterparty-free yield that scales with protocol adoption — and the ecosystem taking shape around it.

---

## Polaris Yield Sources

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/yield/yield-sources

# Polaris Yield Sources

Most stablecoin yield is **rented** from offchain sources — T-bills, CEX lending, custodial strategies — which means counterparty risk and a hard ceiling: your yield is capped at whatever that counterparty delivers. Polaris yield is **owned**. Every source is onchain, internal to the protocol, and grows with usage.

> Adoption ⇒ activity ⇒ velocity ⇒ fees ⇒ yield ⇒ more demand.

## The sources

Source | Where it originates | Flows to

**Borrower interest** | Interest [trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) owners pay on minted pAssets | Primarily [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) depositors; a stewarded split (each floored at 25%) also funds POLAR stakers and the ecosystem fund, and secondary interest rebates pro-rata to troves
**Liquidation gains** | The penalty on liquidated troves, paid in discounted pETH | Stability Pool depositors
**Bonding curve swap fees** | Fees on every ETH ⇄ pETH swap, buffered by the Fee Router and later floor-sold and burned to raise the [floor price](/peth/floor-price) | pETH holders / troves
**Conversion gains** | ETH released when pETH is burned to mint POLAR in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions) | pETH holders / troves
**PolarEX swap fees** | Trading fees from Polaris's integrated DEX, [PolarEX](/core-concepts) | Troves and [POLAR stakers](/stewardship/vepolar)
