The first two drive [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) yield and are the primary venue for pAsset holders. The bonding curve and conversion components make **pETH itself yield-bearing**, which is why using pETH as collateral can lower a borrower's net effective cost.

## Why this is "self-scaling"

None of these sources depends on an external rate environment. They depend on **activity**:

- More troves opened ⇒ more borrower interest.
- More volatility ⇒ more liquidation gains.
- More trading ⇒ more bonding curve and PolarEX swap fees.
- More POLAR minted ⇒ more conversion gains.

Yield is therefore tied to protocol adoption, not to a counterparty's balance sheet. The mechanism scales with pAsset supply, but realized yield still depends on actual activity, rates, liquidations, contract execution, and market conditions. That is what "self-scaling" means: **activity ⇒ yield**, with no offchain dependency to compress or disappear.

## Owned, not rented

Polaris yield is not subsidized, bridged in, or governance-voted into being. It emerges from the protocol's own usage and is captured by the protocol itself — *internalized, not rented*. Because the core is immutable, no admin key can swap these onchain sources for a T-bill desk under pressure; the [degradation pattern](/why-polaris) is architecturally foreclosed.

## How yield is distributed

Onchain fees do not flow straight to recipients: conversion proceeds and swap fees go to the **[Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router)**, which buffers them and distributes pETH yield to troves pro-rata to recorded debt. Routing to [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) lockers is a separate, forthcoming path. (The stewarded primary-interest split that funds the Stability Pool, POLAR stakers, and the ecosystem fund is a distinct, pAsset-denominated stream.) Exact values are finalized closer to launch.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Borrower-interest split | Pending final stewarded bounds and launch value | Determines Stability Pool base yield
Liquidation penalty / discount | Pending final values | Determines event-driven pETH gains for depositors
Bonding-curve swap fee | Pending final audited value | Determines pETH floor growth from swaps
Conversion-auction fee flow | Pending final audited implementation | Determines pETH-holder and trove yield from conversions
PolarEX fee routing | Pending final implementation | Determines split between troves and POLAR stakers
Fee Router cadence and buffers | Pending final implementation | Determines how often yield is distributed and how much is retained as buffer

---

[pUSD: No Counterparty, No Ceiling](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pusd-no-counterparty)
How pUSD delivers counterparty-free yield that scales with protocol adoption — and the ecosystem taking shape around it.

---

## Polaris Redemptions and Liquidations

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/redemptions-liquidations

# Polaris Redemptions and Liquidations

This section explains the mechanisms that keep pAssets backed and near peg. They are not optional background for borrowers or Stability Pool depositors.

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[pAsset Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions) | How below-peg pAssets redeem for pETH collateral, and how that affects every open trove for that pAsset.
[Polaris Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) | When a trove is forced closed and how the Stability Pool or redistribution absorbs it.
[Polaris Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) | What changes when system-wide reserve backing runs thin.

## Launch status

Exact fees, penalties, thresholds, dust rules, keeper incentives, and oracle rules are pending final audited deployment. Use Testnet 2 only for testing, and check [Launch Status](/launch-status) before any production action.

## Related pages

- [Managing a Polaris Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) for borrower controls.
- [Polaris Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) for depositor exposure.
- [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) for the full risk catalog.

---

## Polaris Liquidations

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations

# Polaris Liquidations

> **Before you act:**
> Borrowers: do not manage a trove from the liquidation line. Check the app's current minimum ICR, Recovery Mode status, accrued interest, collateral-price freshness, and gas conditions before withdrawing collateral or minting more debt. Stability Pool depositors: liquidations convert part of your pAsset deposit into pETH exposure.

## What a liquidation is

A **liquidation** closes a trove that has fallen below its **minimum collateralization ratio** — the point at which its collateral no longer comfortably covers its debt. The protocol cancels the trove's debt and seizes its pETH collateral.

The purpose is solvency. Every pAsset is a claim on collateral; leaving undercollateralized troves open would leave the pAsset not fully backed. Liquidations are how Polaris is designed to keep backing intact, subject to functioning contracts, live Ethereum execution, available gas, valid pricing data, keeper/executor availability, and audited invariants.

Unlike a [redemption](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions), a liquidation is not an arbitrage you can opt into at par — it is a forced close triggered by your own ratio crossing a hard line.

## Where the debt and collateral go

Liquidation runs in two tiers.

1. **The Stability Pool absorbs it first.** The [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) holds a reserve of the pAsset. When a trove is liquidated, the pool burns pAsset equal to the trove's debt and receives its pETH collateral in return. Because liquidated troves are seized while still *overcollateralized* (above 100%, below the minimum), the pool typically receives more value in pETH than the pAsset it burns. That surplus is the depositors' compensation.

2. **Redistribution is the fallback.** If the Stability Pool is empty or too small to cover the debt, the trove's debt *and* collateral spread across the remaining troves on that pAsset, in proportion to their size. Other borrowers take on a slice of debt and a matching slice of pETH. The system stays solvent either way.

Path | Trigger | Absorbs debt | Receives collateral

**Stability Pool** | Pool has sufficient pAsset | Pool burns its pAsset | Depositors, pro-rata
**Redistribution** | Pool empty / insufficient | Other troves take it on | Other troves, pro-rata

**Collateral surplus.** When the Stability Pool absorbs a liquidation, the trove surrenders only what covers its debt, a liquidation penalty, and gas compensation for the liquidator. Any collateral beyond that goes to the **Collateral Surplus Pool**, where the former borrower can claim it. (Collateral spread through the redistribution fallback is not returned this way.)

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Minimum ICR per pAsset | Pending final values | Defines when a trove becomes liquidatable in Normal Mode
Liquidation penalty / discount | Pending final values | Determines borrower loss and Stability Pool upside
Gas compensation / keeper incentive | Implemented; final values pending | Affects whether liquidations execute quickly during stress
Stability Pool offset priority | Intended first path; final implementation pending audit | Determines how much debt the pool absorbs before redistribution
Redistribution formula | Pending final implementation | Determines how uncovered debt and collateral spread across remaining troves
Oracle freshness and fallback rules | Pending final audited implementation | Determines whether liquidations pause, continue, or use fallback pricing on stale data
Minimum debt / dust handling | Pending final values | Affects edge cases when troves are nearly closed

## Worked example: liquidation

The numbers below are illustrative only. Suppose the app shows a 120% minimum ICR for a pUSD trove. A borrower has 10,000 pUSD of debt and pETH collateral valued at \$11,800, so the ICR is 118% — below the illustrative minimum, and liquidatable.

If the Stability Pool is large enough, it burns 10,000 pUSD and receives the trove's pETH. The borrower's debt is cleared; the collateral covers the debt, the liquidation penalty, and gas compensation, with any surplus returned for the borrower to claim. Depositors receive pETH pro rata; in a normal overcollateralized liquidation, that pETH should be worth more than the pUSD burned, but the exact gain depends on the final penalty, execution timing, pETH price, gas, and slippage.

If the Stability Pool is empty or too small, the uncovered portion follows the redistribution path: remaining troves inherit a proportional slice of both the liquidated debt and collateral.

## Risks

> **Warning — for borrowers.** Liquidation is involuntary and can happen fast. A liquidated trove gives up its collateral to cover the debt plus a liquidation penalty and gas compensation; any surplus is returned to you to claim, but the penalty makes liquidation a real loss to avoid. Keep a **buffer** above the minimum ratio so a sharp move in collateral price does not cross the line. The thinner your margin, the less room you have when volatility hits. See [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove).

> **Warning — for Stability Pool depositors.** You are the system's first-loss backstop. In a flash crash, troves can be liquidated near — or in extreme cases below — 100% collateralization, and you may receive pETH worth less than the pAsset burned. Over a full cycle the surplus from healthy liquidations compensates for that risk, but it is not riskless yield. See [The Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool) and the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

> **Note:** Exact liquidation thresholds, penalties, and the minimum collateralization ratio per pAsset are finalized closer to launch.

## How liquidation interacts with system health

A trove's individual ratio is not all that matters. When system-wide backing runs thin, the protocol enters [Recovery Mode](/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), which tightens risk-increasing borrower operations while it lasts. Liquidation eligibility is unchanged: a trove is liquidated whenever its ICR falls below the minimum, in every mode. Recovery Mode is the system-level safeguard complementing the trove-level liquidation described here.

---

Liquidations are not punishment — they are the mechanism designed to keep a pAsset backed by the collateral it claims. Keep a buffer, and you are less likely to lose your trove.

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Polaris Recovery Mode

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode

# Polaris Recovery Mode

## The Total Collateralization Ratio

The **Total Collateralization Ratio (TCR)** is the system-wide health metric for a pAsset. It is not a simple market-value-of-collateral over debt figure: it measures the bonding-curve reserve backing all the pETH collateral — the collateral scaled by the curve's reserve-backing fraction and valued at the reserve (redemption) price — divided by total pAsset debt outstanding.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Reserve backing the collateral} \times \text{reserve price}}{\text{Total debt}}
$$

A high TCR means a deep, well-backed system; a low TCR means the reserve behind that pAsset is thin relative to its debt. In the contracts this is the reserve-to-debt ratio, and crossing the threshold triggers the protective state (internally called Emergency Mode).

## What Recovery Mode is

**Recovery Mode** is a protective state the protocol enters automatically when the TCR drops below a set threshold. It is not a freeze and not an emergency shutdown; it tightens the rules to make the system **harder to destabilize** while collateral is scarce.

The purpose is system-level solvency. When the TCR is low, a single sharp move could tip the whole pAsset undercollateralized. Recovery Mode raises the bar for risky behavior so the system pulls back toward health instead of sliding further.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Recovery Mode TCR trigger | Pending final value | Defines when system-wide rules tighten
Recovery Mode exit condition | Pending final implementation | Defines when Normal Mode resumes
Recovery Mode borrower-operation minimum | Pending final per-pAsset value | The minimum ICR collateral-reducing or debt-increasing actions must meet while the protective state is active
Restricted borrower actions | Pending final implementation | Determines which mints, withdrawals, or adjustments are blocked
Fee / incentive changes in Recovery Mode | Pending final launch decision | Affects redeemers, liquidators, and borrower behavior during stress

## How behavior changes

Recovery Mode does **not** change which troves are liquidatable. In every mode, a trove is liquidatable only once its ICR falls below the minimum (the MCR). What tightens is the set of **borrower actions** allowed while the protective state is active.

Your action | Normal Mode | Recovery Mode

**Add collateral / repay debt** | Allowed | Allowed
**Withdraw collateral / mint more debt** | Allowed if ICR stays above the minimum | Allowed only if the resulting ICR clears a higher recovery minimum and does not decrease your ICR
**Liquidation eligibility** | ICR below the minimum | ICR below the minimum (unchanged)

The principle: Recovery Mode restricts actions that would make the system thinner, while always allowing actions that strengthen it. Liquidation eligibility stays exactly where it is in Normal Mode; only risk-increasing operations change, now clearing a higher bar and never lowering your ICR. The net effect pushes every participant toward adding collateral and trimming debt — exactly what raises the system's backing above the threshold and exits Recovery Mode.

> **Note:** The exact threshold that triggers Recovery Mode, and the precise recovery-mode action minimums, are finalized closer to launch. The table above shows the model, not final parameters.

## Worked example: Recovery Mode

The numbers below are illustrative only. The trigger is the system's reserve-backing-to-debt ratio, so a plain collateral-value over debt division is only a rough proxy; treat this as shape, not arithmetic.

Suppose the protective state is active for pUSD. A trove at 130% ICR is **not** liquidated by Recovery Mode: liquidation eligibility is unchanged, so any trove above the minimum stays safe in every mode. What changes is the borrower's freedom to act. While Recovery Mode is active you cannot withdraw collateral or mint more debt unless the result clears the higher recovery minimum and does not lower your ICR; adding collateral and repaying debt are always allowed.

The borrower's defense is the same as always: add collateral, repay debt, or both. For example, a trove with \$13,000 of collateral and 10,000 pUSD of debt is at 130%; repaying 2,000 pUSD moves it to \$13,000 / 8,000 pUSD, or 162.5%, before fees and price movement — and a healthier ICR also widens your room to act while the system is stressed.

## How to make your trove safe

If the system is in or near Recovery Mode, the move is the same one that always protects a trove: raise your **ICR**.

1. **Add pETH collateral.** More collateral against the same debt lifts your ICR directly.
2. **Repay debt.** Burning pAsset to reduce what you owe lifts your ICR from the other side.
3. **Do both early.** Recovery Mode restricts risk-increasing actions and hurts most when you are forced to react under stress, so build the buffer *before* stress arrives, not during it.

See [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) for the mechanics, and [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) for what happens to a trove that falls below the line.

> **Warning:** Recovery Mode is most likely to bite during exactly the volatile conditions when adding collateral is hardest. The only reliable defense is a wide buffer maintained in calm markets. See the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

---

Recovery Mode is the system protecting itself: when backing runs thin, the rules on risk-increasing actions tighten until health returns. Keep your ICR high and your buffer wide, and you keep the freedom to act through the stress.

---

## pAsset Redemptions

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions

# pAsset Redemptions

> **Actionability:** Redemptions are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia using test assets and current manifests. Production redemptions are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production addresses, final fee formula, dust rules, and audit status.

> **Before you act:**
> Redemptions are economic transactions, not guaranteed profit. Check the live fee quote, pETH execution price, gas, expected slippage, possible MEV, and whether the trade stays profitable if conditions change before inclusion. Borrowers should know that a redemption applies collectively and pro-rata across all open troves' collateral, not to a single lowest-ICR trove first, so a below-peg pAsset shrinks every open position carrying that pAsset's debt regardless of its ICR.

## What a redemption is

A **redemption** exchanges a pAsset for the **[pETH](/peth/bonding-curve)** collateral backing it at the pAsset's protocol reference value, minus a fee. It pays when the pAsset trades *below* peg by more than the fee, gas, slippage, and execution risk.

If pUSD slips to \$0.98, a redeemer can buy pUSD cheaply, redeem it for about \$1 of pETH minus the fee, and sell or hold the pETH. The pETH comes from the collateral of troves carrying that pUSD debt, and their debt shrinks by the pUSD redeemed. No counterparty agrees to the trade; the protocol enforces it.

This puts an arbitrage floor under the pAsset's market price.

## Pre-launch parameter status

Parameter | Status | Reader impact

Redemption fee formula | Pending final audited implementation | Determines redeemer net proceeds and final collateral movement
Fee decay / update cadence | Pending final implementation | Affects timing and repeated-redemption profitability
Partial-redemption dust rules | Pending final values | Determines whether tiny trove remnants are allowed or closed
Per-transaction caps / cooldowns | Pending final launch decision | Affects large redemptions and MEV surface
Collateral pricing path | Pending final audited implementation | Determines how much pETH corresponds to the redeemed debt value

## How it restores the peg

A below-peg pAsset is a standing arbitrage. Walk the loop:

1. **Buy** pUSD on the market at \$0.98.
2. **Redeem** it for \$1 − fee of pETH.
3. **Sell** the pETH, pocket the spread.
4. Repeat until the arb closes.

Each pass buys pUSD on the open market, applying **upward price pressure**. Arbitrageurs keep going until pUSD is back at peg and the trade stops paying. Self-interest restores the peg, not intervention.

> **Note:** Redemptions handle the *below*-peg case. The *above*-peg case is handled by the inverse mechanism, **minting**, where arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint pAssets when the pAsset trades over peg. See [Minting pAssets](/minting/minting-passets).

The two mechanisms are mirror images:

Direction | Arbitrageur does | Effect on troves

**Redemption** | pAsset < peg | Burns pAsset (repays debt) ⇒ takes pETH | Debt and collateral both shrink
**Minting** | pAsset > peg | Adds pETH ⇒ mints pAsset | Debt and collateral both grow

Their volumes also serve as the protocol's peg signal: a spike in redemptions reads as a downward depeg, a spike in mintings as an upward one. That is how Polaris sets [autonomous interest rates](/minting/minting-passets) without a direct pUSD market-price feed.

## Mechanics in detail

### Fee formula

The final redemption-fee formula is pending. The user-facing rule is simple: the redeemer receives the redeemed amount's reference value in pETH **minus** the fee and execution costs. If pUSD trades at \$0.98 but the fee, gas, pETH slippage, and MEV risk exceed the \$0.02 spread, the redemption is unprofitable.

The app should show a pre-confirmation quote once the interface is live. Treat examples here as arithmetic only.

### How redemptions are distributed

Redemptions target no specific trove, and there is no sorted ICR queue. A redemption applies **collectively** across every open trove on that pAsset at once. The protocol adjusts a global mint/redeem accounting product, and each trove's share of the redeemed debt and of the pETH removed is proportional to its recorded collateral. A larger trove absorbs a larger absolute share; ICR moves no trove ahead of or behind any other. Adding pETH collateral or repaying debt changes your trove's size, but moves you out of no queue, because none exists.

### Partials

A redemption is sized by the pAsset the redeemer burns, not matched against any single trove. The protocol distributes the debt reduction and the corresponding pETH outflow proportionally across all open troves at once, by each trove's collateral share, in a single system-wide update. The only fill limits: you cannot redeem the entire outstanding debt (the call reverts if the requested pAsset is greater than or equal to total system debt), and the system must stay above its minimum total collateral. Final minimum-debt and dust rules are pending.

### Borrower effects

A redemption repays your debt with your own collateral, so what changes is not your net equity (roughly unchanged at reference prices before fees, rounding, and final fee-routing) but your *control*: your trove shrinks, your pETH exposure shrinks, and your future pETH-linked yield potential falls.

Because redemptions remove equal reference value from both sides of an overcollateralized trove, the remaining ICR often rises after a partial redemption. That does not make redemptions harmless; it makes them an involuntary deleveraging event, not a liquidation penalty.

### MEV and transaction ordering

Redemptions are visible onchain transactions. Searchers can compete for the same spread, the pETH exit price can move, and the system's totals can shift before your transaction lands. Use the app's slippage limits, fee quotes, and any private-transaction support.

## Who gets redeemed against

A redemption applies collectively across **all** open troves carrying that pAsset's debt. Each trove's debt and pETH collateral are reduced in proportion to its recorded collateral, through the protocol's global mint/redeem accounting. There is no per-trove [Individual Collateralization Ratio (ICR)](/resources/glossary) ranking, no "riskiest trove first," and no queue: redemption does not single out the lowest-ICR troves.

Being redeemed against is not a liquidation loss: your debt is repaid at face value with your own collateral, so your net equity is broadly unchanged before fees and rounding. But it is not free either: it closes part of your position involuntarily, at a moment you did not choose.

A higher collateralization ratio protects you against **liquidation**, but does **not** exempt you from redemptions. Every open trove on that pAsset absorbs a proportional share of each redemption regardless of ICR. Because the split is by collateral, a larger trove absorbs a larger absolute amount, but no level of ICR moves you out of range.

> **Note:** Polaris adds borrower-protection details to the naive redemption design. Exact mechanics, including fee routing, dust handling, and any caps or cooldowns, are finalized closer to launch.

## Worked example: redemption impact

Assume pUSD trades at \$0.98, the redemption fee quote is 0.5%, and gas/slippage are ignored. A redeemer buys 10,000 pUSD for \$9,800 and redeems it for \$10,000 of pETH minus the 0.5% fee, or about \$9,950 of pETH. The gross spread is about \$150 before gas, slippage, MEV, and price movement.

Now the borrower side. A 4,000 pUSD redemption applies **collectively**: it reduces total system debt by 4,000 pUSD and removes roughly \$4,000 of pETH from the pool, both reductions shared across every open trove in proportion to its recorded collateral. No single trove has its debt cut from 12,000 to 8,000; each trove absorbs only its proportional slice.

For intuition, here is the proportional share for one trove holding a fifth of the system's collateral, so it absorbs a fifth of the redemption (800 pUSD of debt and about \$800 of pETH):

Step | Collateral value | Debt | ICR

Before redemption | \$18,000 | 12,000 pUSD | 150%
After absorbing its pro-rata share | About \$17,200 | 11,200 pUSD | About 154%

The borrower did not pay off debt voluntarily; the market did it through the redemption path. The trove is slightly safer by ICR, but smaller and less exposed to pETH, as is every other open trove on that pAsset.

## For borrowers

> **Before you act:**
> A redemption can reduce your trove **while it is still solvent**; there is no minimum ICR to cross. Whenever the pAsset trades below peg, every open trove backing it is redeemed against proportionally to its collateral share, shrinking your position and pETH exposure without your consent. ICR governs liquidation safety, not redemption order, so a higher collateralization ratio does not exempt you. See the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

1. Track your **ICR** in absolute terms and keep a comfortable buffer above the MCR; a redemption can touch your position whenever the pAsset is below peg.
2. Redemptions apply pro-rata across all troves backing that pAsset, so there is no ICR queue to climb out of; raising your ICR protects against liquidation, not redemption.
3. Treat a below-peg pAsset as a live signal: that is exactly when redemptions get worked.
4. Interest accrual raises your debt over time, eroding your ICR even if collateral prices are flat.

See [Managing Your Trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) for the levers, and the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) for the full picture.

---

A redemption is the market repricing your pAsset back to par through borrower collateral and arbitrage incentives. It reaches every open trove proportionally, so keep your buffer wide and treat below-peg conditions as the moment your position can shrink.

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Polaris POLAR and Conversion

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/polar

# Polaris POLAR and Conversion

POLAR is the stewardship token. After the initial distribution, new POLAR is intended to enter only when pETH is burned through conversion, not through emissions.

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token) | What POLAR is for and why conversion links it to pETH.
[POLAR Tokenomics](/polar/tokenomics) | Issuance structure, yield routes, vePOLAR, and what is still pending.
[POLAR Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions) | How spike-and-decay pricing, pETH burns, rebates, and floor growth work.
[Participate in a POLAR Conversion](/polar/participate-in-conversion) | The task flow and risks for burning pETH to mint POLAR once the interface is live.

## Launch status

POLAR, conversion, and vePOLAR addresses are pending production publication. Do not use a conversion or locking interface unless [Launch Status](/launch-status) and [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) point to it.

## Related pages

- [vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship](/stewardship/vepolar)
- [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship)
- [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure)

---

## POLAR Conversion Auctions

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/polar/conversion-auctions

# POLAR Conversion Auctions

The conversion auction is the single primitive connecting pETH and POLAR. Converters **burn pETH to mint new POLAR**. It is the only path to new POLAR after the initial distribution, engineered so every conversion strengthens the core protocol.

## Spike-and-decay pricing

Conversion runs as a **continuous, permissionless auction**. Anyone can convert pETH to POLAR at any moment; whether it is *profitable* depends on the conversion price versus POLAR's market price. The price follows a **spike-and-decay** model:

1. The conversion price **continuously decays**. The longer nobody converts, the cheaper the offered price — and the more attractive conversion gets.
2. When a conversion happens, the price **spikes upward**, proportional to the fraction of POLAR supply just inflated. Minting 1% of supply spikes the price far more than minting 0.01%.
3. After the spike, decay resumes until the next conversion.

The result is a natural rhythm: the price decays until an arbitrageur sees it drift below POLAR's market price, converts to capture the spread, and spikes it back up — closing the opportunity until decay reopens it.

> **Note:**
> The spike is computed against **POLAR supply, not pETH supply**. This steers conversions toward small, frequent operations: a whale converting a large amount in one go faces rapidly diminishing returns as each increment spikes the price against them. The mechanism self-regulates toward steady, incremental minting.

In practice, conversions — like pUSD mintings and redemptions — will mostly be executed by arbitrage bots and MEV searchers. The dynamics are tuned for that audience: fast, competitive, self-correcting.

> *Exact decay and spike parameters are finalized closer to launch, set through the protocol's stewardship. The converter rebate is bounded between 0% and 100%; the final bounds for decay and spike are pending audited deployment.*

## What burning pETH does

Burning pETH through a conversion has two structural consequences, on top of minting POLAR:

- **ETH is released from the bonding curve reserve.** The ETH backing the burned pETH is freed. This released ETH is the *conversion gain* — it corresponds to the area under the bonding curve for the burned portion of supply, so the more pETH minted, the more ETH each unit releases.
- **The [floor price](/peth/floor-price) rises.** Reducing supply on a power-law curve while preserving the reserve lifts pETH's enforced minimum. Conversions are strictly one-way — POLAR cannot be converted back to pETH — so the floor can only rise or hold, never fall from conversion activity.

Because the floor only rises, every conversion benefits everyone holding or borrowing against pETH:

Participant | Benefit

pETH holders | Higher floor ⇒ smaller max drawdown
CDP borrowers | Collateral's worst-case value improves ⇒ self-recollateralizing loans get safer
Stability Pool depositors | Healthier system-wide collateralization ratio

This is the second of two floor-raising mechanisms (the other being [swap-fee burns](/peth/bonding-curve)). Swap fees are frequent and lift the floor regardless of POLAR's price; conversions are more sporadic but have larger floor-raising potential.

## The converter rebate

Before the released ETH becomes system yield, a slice can return to the converter. The **converter rebate** is a stewardship-controlled dial between **conversion volume** and **system yield**:

Rebate | Effect

**0%** | Maximum yield to the system; conversions cost the most. Sensible when POLAR's market cap exceeds pETH's.
**100%** | Zero system yield; conversions are cheapest (effective cost set by the [floor ratio](/peth/floor-price)). An emergency lever to drive volume when the floor is stalling.
**In between** | The usual operating range, shifting with market conditions.

The metric stewards watch is the **POLAR / pETH market-cap ratio**: when POLAR's is larger, a low rebate is defensible; when it flips, higher rebates are justified.

> **Note:**
> The total ETH released from the curve is **unaffected** by the rebate. The floor-price impact is identical however the ETH is split afterward. The rebate reshapes the *distribution* of value, not the underlying collateral dynamics.

After the rebate, the remaining ETH is swapped into pETH and forwarded to the **[Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router)**, which buffers it and distributes the yield to pETH troves, pro-rata to their recorded debt.

## What conversion does *not* do

Intellectual honesty requires naming the limits:

- **It does not manufacture demand for POLAR.** If nobody values vePOLAR for yield or stewardship, the arbitrage dries up. When POLAR's market price sits below the conversion price, no conversions happen until decay catches up. The mechanism *assumes* vePOLAR has genuine utility — it does not create it.
- **Parameters carry weight.** Decay too fast ⇒ over-frequent dilution; decay too slow ⇒ stagnant conversion activity. These are set through the stewardship role within bounded ranges — a deliberate reflexivity where stewardship can tune the dilution rate, with final guardrails pending audited deployment.

---

The conversion auction answers "why does POLAR exist?" with a mechanism rather than a promise: POLAR exists because burning pETH to mint it is the most protocol-synergetic action possible. The [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) makes pETH work; the CDP makes pAssets work; the conversion auction makes them work together.

[Burn pETH, Mint POLAR: The Conversion Mechanism](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/burn-pETH-mint-POLAR)
How a single primitive connects Polaris' three engines, and why every conversion strengthens the protocol.

---

## Participate in a POLAR Conversion

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/polar/participate-in-conversion

# Participate in a POLAR Conversion

This is the how-to. For the mechanism behind it, read [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions); for POLAR's role, read [The POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token).

> **Note:**
> Conversions are a continuous, permissionless auction tuned for [arbitrage bots and MEV searchers](/polar/conversion-auctions). Converting profitably means competing with them. Most users will hold POLAR or lock it as [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) instead of converting directly.

> **Actionability:**
> Conversions are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia where the current deployment manifests expose the flow. Production conversions are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) publishes the official app, production conversion contract, final parameters, quote fields, transaction preview, and audit status.

## Before you act

> **Before you act:**
> Conversion is **one-way**: the pETH you burn is gone for good and POLAR cannot be converted back. Your proceeds depend on auction-price and [rebate](/polar/conversion-auctions#the-converter-rebate) dynamics that shift between preview and inclusion, and you compete with bots for the same spread. Convert only on the live previewed quote, never on the examples here. See the [Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure).

Do not attempt a conversion until all of these are true:

- The official app URL is published on [Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status).
- The conversion contract is listed on [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) for the intended network: Sepolia (`11155111`) for Testnet 2, or Ethereum mainnet (`1`) after production launch.
- The conversion price, POLAR market price, rebate, and price-spike preview are visible in the app before signing.
- You are comfortable with a one-way burn: pETH converted to POLAR cannot be converted back.

## What you need

- **pETH** to burn — acquired by buying on the [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) or on a DEX.
- A wallet connected to the Polaris app.

## Steps

### Open the conversion panel

It shows the live **conversion price** and POLAR's **market price** side by side.

### Watch the decaying price

The conversion price decays continuously and spikes upward each time someone converts. A conversion is profitable when it drifts **below** POLAR's market price; that spread is your margin.

### Read the quote

Enter a pETH amount. The panel previews the **POLAR you will receive**, the **ETH rebate** at the current [rebate](/polar/conversion-auctions) setting, and the resulting **price spike**. Because the spike scales with the fraction of POLAR supply minted, larger amounts price progressively worse; favor smaller increments.

### Decide

Convert only while the previewed POLAR (plus rebate) beats acquiring POLAR on the open market. If the spread has closed, wait for decay to reopen it.

### Burn pETH ⇒ receive POLAR

Confirm the transaction. Your pETH is **permanently burned**, you receive newly minted POLAR, and any ETH rebate goes to your wallet.

### Lock or hold

Lock the POLAR as [vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar) to earn yield and stewardship rights, or hold it.

## What just happened

Your single transaction did four things at once:

- Burned pETH ⇒ permanently raised the pETH [floor price](/peth/floor-price).
- Released ETH from the bonding curve reserve.
- Paid you any ETH rebate and routed the rest to the [Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router) as yield.
- Minted you new POLAR.

> **Warning:**
> Conversions are **one-way**. POLAR cannot be converted back to pETH, and burned pETH is gone for good. Confirm the previewed quote before signing.

---

[Burn pETH, Mint POLAR: The Conversion Mechanism](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/burn-pETH-mint-POLAR)
How a single primitive connects Polaris' three engines, and why every conversion strengthens the protocol.

---

## POLAR Token

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/polar/polar-token

# POLAR Token

POLAR is Polaris's stewardship token and the protocol's **growth lever**. It earns that role through one deliberate constraint: after the initial distribution, **new POLAR can only be minted by burning pETH** in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions).

## Why a third token

Polaris runs on three engines: pETH, the pAssets, and POLAR. The first two handle everything the protocol *needs* to function: pETH backs the CDPs, the Stability Pool generates yield, interest rates self-adjust, and liquidations process autonomously. **Polaris works without POLAR.**

So why add a third token? Because functioning and scaling differ. The design splits along an **inward- vs. outward-facing** logic:

Handled by | Examples

**Inward-facing** — sustain the core | pETH + pAssets | interest rates, floor growth, Stability Pool incentivization, liquidations
**Outward-facing** — grow the ecosystem | POLAR | external DEX liquidity, Flow onboarding, [StablecoinOS](/core-concepts) coordination

POLAR is deliberately separated from the core so the core's sustainability never depends on it. If POLAR incentives dried up tomorrow, pUSD would still mint, the bonding curve would still function, and the Stability Pool would still pay yield. The base protocol does not need POLAR to survive, but it scales far better with it.

## Minted only by burning pETH

There is no team mint function and no inflation schedule disconnected from protocol activity. Every POLAR entering circulation after the initial distribution required someone to permanently destroy pETH through the [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions).

This is the point: POLAR's issuance is welded to the **most protocol-synergetic action possible**. Burning pETH simultaneously:

- **Raises the [floor price](/peth/floor-price)** — permanently, by contracting pETH supply.
- **Releases ETH** from the bonding curve reserve, which becomes pETH yield routed through the [Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router).
- **Mints the token** that funds ecosystem growth.

> **Note:**
> Most conversions are executed by arbitrage bots and MEV searchers, not by hand. POLAR issuance therefore tracks real arbitrage activity, not a calendar.

## Productive dilution

Every token emission is dilutive. We will not pretend otherwise — minting new POLAR shrinks existing holders' share of supply. That is a real cost.

The difference is what the dilution *buys*. Each conversion that dilutes POLAR holders also burns pETH, raises the floor, and releases ETH yield. **Every unit of POLAR dilution corresponds to a concrete improvement in protocol fundamentals.** This differs structurally from emission models like CRV, where inflation is disconnected from any protocol benefit.

Conversion is governed by three stewarded parameters: the reserve rebate (bounded to 0–100%), the price-decay half-life, and the supply-inflation spike sensitivity. Issuance is self-limiting: [spike-and-decay pricing](/polar/conversion-auctions#spike-and-decay-pricing) makes large or rapid minting throttle itself, so issuance tracks real arbitrage demand. A hardcoded issuance cap and an explicit disable switch are not yet enforced in the current prototype; final parameter bounds await audited deployment.

For the founding team this means progressive dilution as a *structural guarantee, not a promise*: the faster Polaris grows, the more attractive conversions become, and the more the team's initial share dilutes.

## Locking POLAR: vePOLAR

POLAR on its own is a token. **[vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar)** is that token put to work. Locking POLAR as vePOLAR grants two things:

- **Yield** — a stewarded share of the interest paid by borrowing pAssets.
- **[Stewardship](/stewardship/stewardship)** power — directing quantitative parameters of the StablecoinOS, several constrained by hardcoded bounds. Stewardship steers growth; it does not alter the immutable core.

The loop is self-reinforcing: more conversions ⇒ higher floor + more ETH yield + more potential vePOLAR supply ⇒ more reason to lock ⇒ more stewardship participation.

---

POLAR exists because burning pETH to mint it is the most protocol-synergetic action possible. The token is not a claim on a treasury or a vote in an unbounded DAO — it is a claim on a flywheel whose every turn strengthens the collateral beneath it.

[Burn pETH, Mint POLAR: The Conversion Mechanism](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/burn-pETH-mint-POLAR)
How a single primitive connects Polaris' three engines, and why every conversion strengthens the protocol.

---

## POLAR Tokenomics

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/polar/tokenomics

# POLAR Tokenomics

This page consolidates POLAR's economics. Each piece has its own page; here they sit side by side. For the reasoning behind the token, read [The POLAR Token](/polar/polar-token); for the issuance mechanism, [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions).

> **Current status:** POLAR's total supply, distribution schedule, and vesting terms are pending and will be published at launch. This page describes the *structure* of POLAR's economics, fixed by design, not the final numbers, which are forthcoming. Treat any figure not stated here as not yet finalized.

## Issuance: minted only by burning pETH

After the initial distribution, **new POLAR can be minted only by burning pETH** in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions). There is no team mint function and no calendar-based inflation schedule.

- Every POLAR that enters circulation after launch required someone to permanently destroy pETH.
- Conversion runs as a continuous, permissionless [spike-and-decay auction](/polar/conversion-auctions), so issuance tracks real arbitrage activity, not a fixed emission curve. Most conversions are executed by arbitrage bots and MEV searchers.

This welds POLAR's issuance to the most protocol-synergetic action available: each conversion simultaneously [raises the floor price](/peth/floor-price), releases ETH from the bonding-curve reserve as yield, and mints the POLAR.

## A bounded, productive dilution

Every conversion dilutes existing POLAR holders, but the dilution is bounded and buys a concrete improvement:

- **Bounded.** Conversion issuance is self-limiting — [spike-and-decay pricing](/polar/conversion-auctions) makes large or rapid minting throttle itself, so issuance tracks real arbitrage demand. Stewards tune the conversion-price parameters (spike sensitivity, half-life, and the reserve rebate); a hardcoded issuance cap and an explicit disable switch are not yet enforced in the current prototype, and final parameter bounds are pending audited deployment.
- **Productive.** Each unit of dilution corresponds to burned pETH, a higher floor, and released ETH yield, structurally unlike emission models where inflation is disconnected from any protocol benefit.
- **Progressive for the team.** The founding team's outsized early share is diluted by the same mechanism: the faster Polaris grows, the more attractive conversions become, and the more that initial share shrinks. The dilution is enforced by code, not pledged in a forum.

## vePOLAR: vote-escrow

POLAR alone is a token; **[vePOLAR](/stewardship/vepolar)** is that token locked to work. Locking POLAR for a period returns vePOLAR, which grants:

- **A share of protocol yield** — funded by protocol revenue, not minted as emissions.
- **[Stewardship](/stewardship/stewardship) rights** — tuning quantitative parameters within hardcoded bounds. vePOLAR *stewards*; it does not govern, and it cannot reach the immutable core.

Lock durations, vote-power decay, unlock rules, and the final yield split are pending audited deployment.

## Where the yield comes from

POLAR's value accrual runs on hard-asset protocol revenue, buffered and routed by the **[Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router)** (pETH) and, separately, by the protocol's interest router (pAsset). It reaches three destinations:

Destination | What they receive

**Troves** | pETH yield from the Fee Router, credited pro-rata to recorded debt
**POLAR stakers (vePOLAR)** | A stewarded share of protocol interest, in pAsset terms (forthcoming)
**Flow recipients** | Revenue-sharing for whitelisted contracts via [Flows](/stewardship/flows) (forthcoming)

Inputs are hard assets from protocol activity (conversions and bonding-curve swap fees), not emitted POLAR. The Fee Router buffers this pETH and pushes it to a stewardship-chosen receiver, today the troves; the bounded split with hardcoded floors that funds stakers and the ecosystem applies to the separate pAsset primary-interest stream. Exact destination splits and their bounds are pending audited deployment.

## What POLAR does *not* have

The structure deliberately omits the usual extraction surfaces:

- **No gauges.** Stewardship cannot create POLAR emissions gauges.
- **No bribes.** No vote-buying markets are routed through the protocol.
- **No emissions.** Ecosystem incentives flow through [Flows](/stewardship/flows) as hard-asset revenue sharing, funded by protocol revenue, not token emissions.
- **No mint outside conversion.** POLAR cannot be minted by any path other than burning pETH.

## The Polar Council

The **Polar Council** is a treasury-funded program for onboarding and ecosystem contributors (**8.2% of POLAR supply**), comparable in spirit to veAERO's Flight School. Initially team-managed; its operating framework, eligibility, and reporting requirements are pending publication.

## Numbers that are pending

The economic *structure* above is fixed by design. The following are not yet finalized and must not be assumed:

Item | Status

Total / initial POLAR supply | Pending
Distribution schedule | Pending
Vesting terms | Pending
Conversion rate and parameter bounds | Pending (parameter bounds forthcoming)
Destination splits across yield recipients | Pending
Lock durations and vote-power decay | Pending

---

**[Go deeper →](https://polarisfinance.io/blog)**

---

## Polaris Stewardship

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/stewardship

# Polaris Stewardship

Stewardship is the bounded human layer around an immutable core. It can tune approved parameters and onboard participants within hardcoded constraints; it cannot rewrite pETH, CDP, liquidation, redemption, or Stability Pool logic.

## Read in order

Page | What it answers

[Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship) | What stewards can and cannot change.
[vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship](/stewardship/vepolar) | How POLAR locking is intended to grant stewardship rights and routed yield.
[Polaris Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router) | How pETH revenue is buffered and distributed.
[Polaris Flows](/stewardship/flows) | How ecosystem revenue sharing is intended to replace token-emission incentives.

## Launch status

Stewardship contracts, lock terms, voting parameters, Flow whitelists, event signatures, and final bounds are pending audited deployment. Check [Launch Status](/launch-status) before treating any vote or parameter as live.

## Related pages

- [POLAR Tokenomics](/polar/tokenomics) for issuance and yield structure.
- [Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) for artifact status.
- [Audits & Security](/resources/audits-security) before relying on the stewardship surface.

---

## Polaris Fee Router

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/stewardship/fee-router

# Polaris Fee Router

The Fee Router is the plumbing that turns protocol activity into distributed yield. Onchain fees do not flow straight to recipients; they pass through the router, which buffers them and routes proceeds under immutable and stewarded constraints.

> **Current status:** The Fee Router address, ABI, event catalog, buffering parameters, distribution cadence, claim path, and final split bounds are pending audited deployment. This page describes the intended routing model without inventing live parameters.

## What goes in

The Fee Router receives pETH-denominated value from protocol activity:

- **Conversions.** When pETH is burned to mint POLAR in a [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions), released ETH can be swapped back into pETH after any converter rebate and forwarded to the router.
- **Bonding-curve swap fees.** Fees charged on ETH/pETH activity can feed pETH yield paths according to the final contract design.
- **Other protocol revenue.** Any additional onchain fees routed through the Fee Router at launch will be listed in the contract reference and yield docs.

Inputs are hard assets from protocol activity, not emitted POLAR.

## What comes out

The router distributes yield according to published rules and bounds:

Destination | What they receive | Status

**Troves** | Yield credited to pETH collateral or the relevant accounting path | Accounting path pending
**POLAR stakers** | A stewarded share of distributed protocol yield | Final split and claim path pending
**Flow recipients** | Revenue-sharing distributions for whitelisted contracts, where applicable | Flow deployment and parameters pending

The Fee Router itself does not enforce a per-destination split: [stewardship](/stewardship/stewardship) pushes buffered pETH rewards to a chosen receiver, bounded by the accrued buffer and the immutable buffering cadence. The bounded, stewarded split with hardcoded floors applies to primary interest (the pAsset-denominated revenue stream) via the separate [interest router](/yield/yield-sources), not the Fee Router's pETH rewards. Either way, the router moves only existing assets and cannot create token emissions.

## Why buffer at all

The Fee Router buffers incoming pETH rather than paying it out the instant it arrives. Conversions and swap fees can be bursty: sporadic in timing, uneven in size. Buffering smooths that inflow into steadier yield, so recipients see accounting less dependent on single spikes.

Exact buffer size, cadence, and distribution mechanics are launch parameters and remain pending.

## The converter rebate

The Fee Router sits downstream of one of stewardship's key dials: the **converter rebate**. Before released ETH becomes system yield, a stewarded slice can be returned to the converter.

- **Higher rebate** -> conversions are cheaper -> more conversion volume, less yield reaching the router.
- **Lower rebate** -> conversions cost more -> less volume, more yield reaching the router.

It is a dial between conversion volume and system yield — it reshapes how that value splits, not the total ETH released or the floor-price impact. See [Conversion Auctions](/polar/conversion-auctions#the-converter-rebate) for the full mechanism.

## Operator and integrator prerequisites

Before relying on the Fee Router, operators and integrators should verify:

- Fee Router address, source, and ABI.
- The published list of input sources.
- The current recipient split and its min/max bounds.
- Distribution cadence and claim path.
- Events for deposits, buffering, distributions, claims, and parameter changes.
- Whether a Flow recipient is whitelisted, what address is observed, what address receives yield, and what weight applies.
- Any audit notes specific to router accounting.

## Indexing considerations

Indexers should model the router as a stateful distribution system, not a simple transfer log:

- Track inputs separately by source.
- Track buffered amounts before distribution.
- Track each distribution or claim by recipient type.
- Store the parameter values active during each distribution.
- Reconcile aggregate indexed totals against contract reads when the ABI is published.

## Where it sits in the system

The Fee Router is one piece of a larger picture. For every onchain yield source on Polaris, see [Where the Yield Comes From](/yield/yield-sources). For Flow-specific routing, see [Flows](/stewardship/flows).

---

[pUSD: No Counterparty, No Ceiling](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pusd-no-counterparty)
How pUSD delivers counterparty-free yield that scales with protocol adoption — and the ecosystem taking shape around it.

---

## Polaris Flows

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/stewardship/flows

# Polaris Flows

Ecosystem incentivization is where most tokenomics go to die. The usual pattern: a DAO emits its native token, farmers capture it, dump it on long-term holders, and TVL evaporates when emissions stop. Backroom deals decide who gets subsidized.

**Flows are Polaris's replacement.** They distribute protocol revenue to contracts building on Polaris, denominated in hard assets such as pETH and pAssets, funded by perpetual revenue, and allocated by an auditable formula. No gauges, no bribes, no backroom deals, no emissions.

> **Current status:** Flow contracts, whitelists, observed-address rules, recipient rules, weights, fixed-minimum kickstarts, event signatures, and final parameter bounds are pending audited deployment. This page describes the intended model and operator requirements.

## The base rule

A whitelisted contract receives a Flow equal to its share of the underlying asset, relative to all other whitelisted contracts.

$$
\text{Flow share} = \frac{\text{contract's weighted asset held}}{\text{total weighted asset held across all whitelisted contracts}}
$$

Hold 20% of the whitelisted pETH supply -> receive 20% of the pETH Flow, before any launch-specific adjustments. Holdings vote with their wallets; the formula does the rest.

## Two Flow families

Flow | Recipients | Funded by | Status

**pETH Flow** | Eligible pETH-holding contracts such as Polaris CDPs or external integrations | Bonding-curve and conversion-related protocol revenue, subject to final routing | Pending
**pAsset Flow** | Eligible pAsset-holding contracts such as liquidity pools, vaults, markets, or structured products | Borrower interest or other pAsset revenue after the Stability Pool allocation, which sits above a hardcoded floor | Pending

Each Flow has its own whitelist and parameters. The final contract reference will define which assets, recipients, and revenue sources go live at launch.

## Immutable by default, stewarded at the edges

The Flow implementation is intended to be immutable. In normal operation, stewards do not vote every epoch or steer a gauge; the formula computes distributions from eligible holdings.

vePOLAR's role is confined to the edges:

- **Whitelist.** A contract must be admitted by a stewardship vote before it can receive Flow revenue.
- **Weight.** A whitelisting can carry a bounded weight multiplier on top of raw ownership share.
- **Recipient.** A whitelist entry can distinguish the observed address from the address that receives revenue, if the final contracts support that separation.
- **Removal.** A participant that games the system can be removed per the published process.

> **Parameter status:** Whitelist fields, weight bounds, kickstart mechanics, Stability Pool floors, and removal rules are pending final publication. They are stewarded only within immutable bounds.

## Operator prerequisites

Teams seeking Flow participation should prepare:

- Product description and target users.
- The asset held: pETH or a specific pAsset.
- Observed contract address and recipient address, once deployed.
- Verified source links and ABI for the operator's own contracts.
- Upgrade/admin model for those contracts.
- How user deposits create durable Polaris asset demand.
- Requested Flow weight or kickstart, if applicable, with the published bounds shown.
- Monitoring plan, including how holdings and revenue are reported.
- Risk disclosure for users of the operator's product.

Final application templates and acceptance criteria are pending. Operators should not assume Flow eligibility until approved through the published stewardship process.

## Flow lifecycle

Stage | What happens | Status

**Application** | Operator submits product, contracts, requested Flow, and risk details | Template pending
**Review** | Stewards and reviewers check eligibility, security posture, and parameter bounds | Process pending
**Vote** | vePOLAR votes on whitelist admission and bounded settings | Voting details pending
**Activation** | Approved recipient is added to the whitelist via the official execution path | Contract path pending
**Distribution** | Formula allocates revenue by holdings, weights, and active parameters | Events pending
**Monitoring** | Holdings, distributions, and risks are tracked over time | Reporting pending
**Removal or adjustment** | Stewards can remove or adjust entries inside published bounds | Process pending

## Flows vs token emissions

The structural contrast is the reason Flows exist:

Aspect | Emission gauges | Polaris Flows

**Currency** | Governance token, emitted and dilutive | Hard assets: pETH and pAssets
**Funding** | Constant token emissions | Perpetual protocol revenue
**Allocation** | Gauge votes, bribes, and recurring weight wars | Share of asset held, auto-computed
**Recipient** | Whoever farms the gauge fastest | Contracts whose users hold productive Polaris assets
**Stewardship load** | Epoch votes and lobbying | One onboarding vote plus bounded adjustments
**Sustainability** | Dries up when emissions end | Scales with protocol activity

The only way new POLAR enters existence after inception is a [pETH burn](/polar/conversion-auctions). Flows keep ecosystem growth aligned with that constraint.

## Where Flows sit in the flywheel

Flows close the loop: deposits bring pETH, pETH backs CDPs, CDPs pay interest, interest feeds revenue-sharing paths, and those paths make integrators want to capture more pETH and pAssets. Growth in any part of the system can feed yield to other parts without a single POLAR emitted.

![The Polaris flywheel](/infographics/polaris-flywheel.svg)

By default, Flows should just work. Stewards step in only where human judgment is load-bearing: onboard a participant, set bounded terms, or remove one that no longer qualifies.

---

[Sustaining the Ecosystem: pETH & pUSD Flows](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-flows)
How Polaris replaces emission-style incentives with an autonomous, hard-asset distribution system to sustain the pETH and pAssets ecosystems.

---

## Polaris Stewardship, not Governance

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/stewardship/stewardship

# Polaris Stewardship, not Governance

Governance doesn't fail because of bad people; it fails because of **bad structure**. Unlimited scope, undefined boundaries, and misaligned incentives eventually turn a DAO into an extraction arena, whoever is at the table.

Polaris's answer is neither a fully immutable protocol with no adjustable parameters nor an unbounded DAO. It is a third path: an immutable core plus a clearly scoped layer where humans exercise judgment only where judgment is load-bearing. We call it **stewardship** rather than governance because the difference is structural, not cosmetic.

> **Current status:** Stewardship is not live yet. POLAR and vePOLAR contract addresses, lock terms, proposal thresholds, voting periods, execution delays, and final numeric parameter bounds await audited deployment. These docs describe the intended scope and lifecycle without inventing launch parameters.

## The core is immutable

The mechanisms that define Polaris **cannot be changed by anyone**: not the team, not vePOLAR, not a vote. They are enforced by immutable contracts:

- The [bonding curve](/peth/bonding-curve) backing pETH
- CDP logic and [trove](/minting/managing-your-trove) accounting
- [Liquidations](/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations)
- [Redemptions](/redemptions-liquidations/redemptions)
- The [Stability Pool](/yield/stability-pool)

The Polaris you understand on day one is the Polaris you hold later. There is no re-learning the base protocol after each vote, because the load-bearing logic is out of reach by design.

## What stewardship controls

Stewardship operates at the edges: StablecoinOS onboarding, permitted parameter dials, the POLAR stewardship layer, fee and interest splits, Flow participation, and treasury programs. Safety-critical adjustable values ship with hardcoded limits; the remaining numeric bounds are set at audited deployment.

Domain | Power | Constraint | Launch status

**pAsset licensing** | Approve a team or instance to deploy a new pAsset on Polaris infrastructure | Standardized eligibility and launch requirements | Process pending
**CDP parameters** | Adjust permitted fee and distribution splits for a pAsset instance | Hardcoded min/max bounds | Numeric bounds pending
**Conversion parameters** | Tune pETH-to-POLAR conversion settings (rebate, half-life, spike sensitivity) | Rebate bounded 0–100%; other bounds pending | Numeric bounds pending
**StablecoinOS onboarding** | Whitelist eligible participants into Flow revenue sharing | Must build on pETH/pAssets and meet published requirements | Process pending
**Primary interest split** | Set how primary interest is split between the Stability Pool and POLAR stakers (remainder to the ecosystem fund) | Stability Pool and stakers each floored at 25% (hardcoded) | Floors hardcoded; values pending
**Flow weights** | Set whitelisted recipient weights and related edge parameters | Hardcoded limits; no gauges, bribes, or emissions | Numeric bounds pending
**Treasury programs** | Operate programs such as the Polar Council | Initially team-managed until a framework is published | Framework pending

A stewardship vote can bring a new pAsset or Flow participant into the system, or move an approved parameter within its range. It cannot alter the bonding curve, redemption mechanism, liquidation logic, Stability Pool mechanics, or core contract immutability.

## What stewardship does not do

Polaris stewardship does **not**:

- Upgrade or replace immutable core contracts.
- Add admin keys, pause powers, freeze powers, or blacklists.
- Create POLAR emissions gauges.
- Route bribes or vote-buying markets through the protocol.
- Mint POLAR outside the pETH conversion mechanism.
- Set safety-critical parameters (such as interest-rate maxima, interest-split floors, and fee or rebate ceilings) outside their hardcoded bounds.
- Change a user's trove, Stability Pool deposit, pETH balance, or lock by discretion.

## Operator prerequisites

Teams seeking onboarding, a pAsset instance, Flow participation, or another stewarded action should expect to provide:

- Clear contract addresses once deployed and verified.
- A concise description of the product, risk surface, and user flow.
- The exact stewarded action requested.
- The parameter values requested, with the published min/max bounds shown beside them.
- Evidence that the action cannot touch immutable core logic.
- Security status: audits, tests, admin roles, upgradeability, and emergency controls for the requesting team's own contracts.
- Revenue-sharing, Flow, or integration assumptions, if relevant.
- Monitoring and reporting commitments after approval.

The final submission template is pending. Until it is published, operators should prepare around these fields rather than assume an informal path.

## Proposal and voting lifecycle

The final stewardship UI and contract-level process are pending. The intended lifecycle is:

Stage | Purpose | Status

**Draft** | Describe the request, affected contracts, user impact, risks, and requested parameters | Template pending
**Bounds check** | Confirm each requested value sits inside immutable min/max limits | Final bounds pending
**Security review** | Confirm verified source, audit status, admin roles, and integration risks | Process pending
**Vote** | vePOLAR holders vote according to the published lock and vote-power rules | Thresholds and periods pending
**Queue / execution** | Approved changes are queued and executed through the published contract path | Timelock/execution details pending
**Verification** | Docs, indexers, and monitors confirm the new state onchain | Artifact plan pending
**Monitoring** | Operators and stewards watch the result and propose reversals or adjustments if needed | Reporting framework pending

No proposal is executable until the official process, contracts, and parameter bounds are published.

## The three-tier decision model

Polaris follows a simple ordering: solve a problem at the lowest tier that can solve it, and escalate only what the tier below cannot handle.

1. **Automate what you can.** Interest-rate setting, floor-price growth, liquidation processing, and redemptions run autonomously.
2. **Incentivize what you can't automate.** Where behavior can't be hardcoded but can be paid for, durable protocol revenue and Flow formulas handle it.
3. **Steward only what's left.** Only what genuinely requires qualitative judgment goes to vePOLAR.

This ordering reserves vePOLAR attention for the few decisions where human discernment matters.

## Bounded blast radius

Stewards hold real power, so they can make real mistakes, but the immutable core caps the damage. License a weak pAsset that fails? It affects that instance and its marginal share of the system, not the bonding curve or liquidation engine. Set a poor split? It can be moved back inside the same hardcoded bounds. The design absorbs errors instead of letting them rewrite the protocol.

## How this compares to DAO governance

A structural comparison, not an attack. Every team named below shipped real, valuable work; the point is the system's shape, not the people in it.

Unbounded DAO governance | Polaris stewardship

**Scope** | Open-ended; can reach the base protocol | Enumerated and bounded
**Core** | Mutable via vote | Immutable, out of reach of any vote
**Incentives** | Often routed through emissions, gauges, and bribes | Routed through protocol revenue, configurable revenue splits, and Flows
**Failure mode** | Base-protocol denaturation, capture, extraction | Bounded mistakes at the edges

- **Maker** repeatedly denatured its base protocol, chasing trends and creating bespoke markets that benefited insiders; unanchored scope is the enabling condition.
- **Aave** carried sizable undisclosed conflicts of interest; a proposal to enforce basic disclosure was rejected by entities that themselves failed to disclose. The system resisted its own cleanup.
- **Uniswap** protects its base protocol, yet unscoped governance over the UNI token still produced familiar extraction. Even a protected core invites extraction when surrounding scope is unbounded.

The lesson Polaris draws: bound the scope first, and most of these failure modes lose their oxygen. Less to capture, less to overpay for, nothing to denature.

## Structure over trust

Stewardship is not a promise to behave well; it is a structure in which misbehavior has a small, bounded surface. The core is immutable. Each lever is bounded, auditable, and reversible where the underlying contract permits. The team's outsized early influence is progressively diluted through the [conversion mechanism](/polar/conversion-auctions): the faster Polaris grows, the faster new POLAR enters circulation and the team's share shrinks. That dilution is enforced by code, not pledged in a forum.

---

[Stewardship, not Governance](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/stewardship-not-governance)
Governance fails because of structure, not people. Here's how stewardship fixes the structure.

---

## vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship

URL: https://docs.polarisfinance.io/stewardship/vepolar

# vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship

**vePOLAR is vote-escrowed POLAR.** Lock [POLAR](/polar/polar-token) and receive vePOLAR: the right to steward bounded parameters and participate in the protocol's edge decisions.

> **Current status:** The vePOLAR locking interface, contract address, lock durations, vote-power decay, unlock rules, proposal thresholds, and final yield split are pending audited deployment. Do not lock POLAR through an unofficial interface.

## What vePOLAR does

```
lock POLAR -> receive vePOLAR -> steward bounded parameters + receive routed yield
```

vePOLAR holders are **stewards, not governors**. vePOLAR steers quantitative parameters within hardcoded ranges and votes on permitted onboarding decisions. It cannot reach the immutable core: the bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, redemptions, and Stability Pool mechanics are beyond any vote.

vePOLAR holders can participate in decisions to:

- License new pAssets onto Polaris infrastructure.
- Onboard eligible participants into StablecoinOS and [Flow](/stewardship/flows) revenue sharing.
- Set bounded interest splits and CDP parameters.
- Tune [conversion](/polar/conversion-auctions) parameters within their hardcoded range.
- Vote on treasury programs once the framework is published.

Each action is bounded, auditable, and limited to the surfaces in [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship). None can denature the protocol.

## What vePOLAR does not do

vePOLAR cannot upgrade core contracts; change pETH, CDP, liquidation, redemption, or Stability Pool mechanics; create gauges or bribe markets; direct POLAR emissions; mint POLAR outside the [conversion auction](/polar/conversion-auctions); or move any parameter outside its immutable min/max range. The full list of what no vote can reach is in [Stewardship, not Governance](/stewardship/stewardship#what-stewardship-does-not-do).

## Lock status

The final lock design is pending. At launch, this page will list:

Field | Status

POLAR contract address | Pending
vePOLAR contract address | Pending
Network | Testnet 2: Sepolia; production: Ethereum mainnet after launch
Lock duration choices | 1 week to 4 years
Vote-power formula and decay | Linear decay
Increase/extend | Yes, anytime
Withdraw | At lock expiry
Delegation | Yes
Yield claim path | Pending

## Yield for lockers

vePOLAR is not influence alone. Lockers are one destination of distributed protocol yield: a stewarded share of protocol interest is routed to POLAR stakers, alongside the pETH the [Fee Router](/stewardship/fee-router) distributes to troves.

The exact split, buffering, and claim mechanics are pending final deployment. This is routed protocol revenue, not POLAR emissions.

## Prerequisites for locking

Before locking POLAR, verify:

- The official app URL.
- POLAR and vePOLAR addresses against [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts).
- Verified source and ABI against [Contracts and Addresses](/resources/contracts).
- Lock duration, voting power, unlock, and withdrawal rules.
- The final yield split and claim path.
- The audit status of the locking and stewardship contracts.

Locking is an illiquid commitment by design. Do not lock through any contract or frontend not published in official Polaris docs.

## The Polar Council

The **Polar Council** is a treasury-funded program to bootstrap aligned, active stewards. Comparable in spirit to veAERO's Flight School, it is a structured way to cultivate participants who understand the protocol and contribute to its stewardship, rather than leaving the role to whoever shows up.

> **Current status:** The Council's operating framework is initially team-managed and will be formalized as providers step up and launch approaches. Exact terms, eligibility, and reporting requirements are pending.

---

[Stewardship, not Governance](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/stewardship-not-governance)
Governance fails because of structure, not people. Here's how stewardship fixes the structure.

# Polaris Resources

Page | What it answers

[Polaris Risk Disclosure](/resources/risk-disclosure) | The risk catalog for smart contracts, collateral, liquidations, Stability Pool deposits, MEV, phishing, and Ethereum dependency.

[Polaris Contracts & Addresses](/resources/contracts) | Deployment shape, address status, and verification rules.
[Polaris Audits & Security](/resources/audits-security) | Audit-report status, security model, and disclosure/bounty status.
[Polaris Brand Assets](/resources/brand-assets) | Logos, palette, typography, and usage guidance.

## Launch status

[Polaris Launch Status](/launch-status) is the canonical source for current network, app, contract, audit, and bounty status. Resources pages should be read through that lens until production mainnet details are published.
